name: GI Stasis
cause: Gut motility slows or stops. Most common triggers (in order): (1) Low-fiber diet — pellet-heavy with insufficient hay. (2) Pain from any source — dental disease, surgery, bladder stones, post-spay/neuter. (3) Stress — new pet, moving, loud noise. (4) Dehydration. (5) Inappropriate antibiotic (PLACE rule). The slowed gut allows abnormal bacteria to overgrow and produce gas, which is painful → further appetite loss → vicious cycle.
script: GI stasis means the gut has stopped moving. The single most common cause is diet — rabbits, guinea pigs, and chinchillas need lots of hay (at least 70% of what they eat) to keep the gut moving. Pain anywhere else in the body — teeth, bladder, even stress — can also slow the gut down. Sometimes it just happens with no clear cause. With early treatment most pets recover well, which is why we need to start fluids, pain meds, and force-feeding right now.
preventableNote: About 80% trace to insufficient hay or hidden dental disease. The remaining 20% come from pain sources the owner couldn't reasonably see (e.g. early bladder stones, hidden cheek-tooth spurs).
prognosis: Good if treated within 12–24h. Mortality climbs sharply past 48h of anorexia.
prevention: 70%+ of diet = unlimited grass hay (Timothy, Orchard, Meadow). Limit pellets to ¼ cup per 2 kg per day. NO seeds, nuts, grains, bread. Fruit ≤ 1 tbsp/day as a treat. Daily exercise outside the cage. Dental check every 6–12 months. Target dietary fibre ADF ≥ 30% (the ~70%-hay diet) for normal 4–6 h gut transit — low-fibre pellet diets (ADF 10–16%) measurably slow the gut and raise AST + plasma histamine BEFORE overt stasis appears.

name: Dental Disease / Malocclusion
cause: Rabbit, GP, and chinchilla teeth grow continuously throughout life — about 2–3 mm per week. They are normally worn down by chewing fibrous hay. When the diet is pellet- or treat-heavy, the teeth aren't worn evenly and develop sharp spurs that cut the tongue and cheek, causing pain and drooling. Genetic skull conformation (e.g. lop-eared rabbits, dwarf breeds) can also predispose. Trauma from chewing cage bars or falls can fracture a tooth and start the same cycle.
script: Their teeth grow continuously — like your fingernails. The only thing that wears them down properly is chewing tough hay. When the diet leans toward pellets or soft treats, the teeth grow uneven and form sharp points that cut the tongue. That's what's causing the drooling and reduced appetite. Some breeds — lop-eared rabbits especially — are genetically predisposed regardless of diet. Today we'll grind the spurs down under anaesthesia, but for long-term prevention the diet must shift to mostly hay.
preventableNote: 70% diet-related — preventable with proper hay. 30% genetic predisposition (lop breeds, dwarf breeds, brachycephalic conformation) — not preventable but manageable with q3–6 month dental checks.
prognosis: Manageable with periodic crown reduction (q1–6 months depending on severity). Some progress to root abscesses (poor prognosis).
prevention: Hay-first diet. Limit pellets. Avoid wooden chew toys that are too soft. Annual dental exam — earlier if predisposed breed.

name: Encephalitozoonosis (E. cuniculi)
cause: Caused by *Encephalitozoon cuniculi*, a microsporidian parasite that most rabbits are exposed to as kits from their mother (transplacental + via milk). It hides in the kidneys, brain, and eye for years without causing symptoms. When the rabbit's immunity drops (stress, age, concurrent illness), the parasite reactivates and damages whichever organ it has settled in — most commonly the vestibular system (head tilt), kidneys (chronic renal disease), or lens of the eye (cataract).
script: This is caused by a tiny parasite called E. cuniculi that almost all rabbits carry from birth — it came from their mother. It usually sleeps quietly in the body for years. When the immune system gets stressed — old age, another illness, even a big change at home — it can wake up and attack the brain, kidneys, or eye. That's what's causing the head tilt today. You didn't do anything wrong; this was already inside the rabbit.
preventableNote: Almost all pet rabbits in Asia carry E. cuniculi by 6 months of age. You cannot prevent exposure. You can reduce reactivation risk by minimizing stress and treating concurrent illness early.
prognosis: 80% of head-tilt cases improve significantly with prompt fenbendazole + supportive care. 20% retain permanent head tilt but live normal lives. Renal form has guarded prognosis.
prevention: Fenbendazole 20 mg/kg PO q24h × 28 days at first sign of symptoms. Some breeders use prophylactic 9-day course annually. Minimize chronic stress.

name: Pasteurellosis (Snuffles)
cause: *Pasteurella multocida* is a bacterium that colonizes the upper airway of most healthy rabbits without causing disease. When the immune system is weakened (stress, poor diet, ammonia buildup from dirty litter, sudden temperature change), the bacterium overgrows and causes sneezing, nasal discharge, eye discharge, and eventually pneumonia or head tilt (inner ear infection). Highly contagious between rabbits via direct contact and shared bowls.
script: Pasteurella is a bacterium that lives in the noses of most healthy rabbits, harmlessly. It only causes disease when the rabbit gets stressed or run-down — poor diet, dirty cage, or sudden cold drafts can trigger it. Once it takes hold, it tends to come back throughout life — like a chronic sinus infection in a human. Today we'll start antibiotics and address the husbandry to give your rabbit the best chance.
preventableNote: Cannot prevent exposure (carried by most rabbits). Can reduce reactivation: stable temperature, clean litter (low ammonia), good diet, quarantine new rabbits 30+ days.
prognosis: Acute episode responds well to enrofloxacin or TMS. Tends to relapse — many rabbits need recurring courses. Pneumonia or inner ear involvement = guarded.
prevention: Clean litter daily (ammonia damages the airway). Stable temperature, no drafts. Avoid stress. Quarantine new rabbits ≥ 30 days. Probiotics during antibiotic courses.

name: Uterine Adenocarcinoma
cause: A spontaneous cancer of the uterine lining that develops in intact female rabbits. By age 5, 50–80% of unspayed does will develop it. The cause is hormonal — lifelong exposure to estrogen drives the change. There is no infectious or dietary cause. It is the single biggest medical reason intact female rabbits die prematurely.
script: This is a cancer of the uterus that affects almost all unspayed female rabbits as they age. By age 5, more than half are affected; by 7, almost all. The cause is purely hormonal — there's nothing you did or fed her that caused it. This is exactly why we strongly recommend spaying female rabbits at 6 months — it removes the uterus and ovaries before cancer ever has a chance to start.
preventableNote: Almost 100% preventable by spaying at 4–6 months old. Once cancer develops, OVH is still curative if pre-metastatic.
prognosis: Excellent if caught early and OVH done before metastasis. Once it spreads to lungs (common — uses lymphatic + hematogenous route), prognosis is grave.
prevention: Elective ovariohysterectomy (spay) at 4–6 months of age. For older intact does: annual abdominal palpation + ultrasound.

name: Sore Hocks (Pododermatitis)
cause: Pressure necrosis of the skin over the hock from prolonged contact with hard or wire flooring, combined with obesity, urine soiling, or thin foot fur (Rex breed). The skin breaks down, opportunistic bacteria infect, and a chronic ulcer forms. Each step is painful, so the rabbit shifts weight to other feet → those break down too.
script: Bumblefoot is caused by pressure on the bottom of the feet. Wire cage floors, wet bedding, being overweight, or genetics in some breeds (Rex rabbits don't have fur on their feet) — all make it worse. Once a sore starts, bacteria infect it and it becomes painful, which is why your rabbit is reluctant to move. Today we'll bandage and start antibiotics, but the long-term fix is changing the cage floor to solid surface with soft bedding.
preventableNote: Highly preventable with solid floor + soft bedding + weight management.
prognosis: Mild–moderate cases heal in 4–8 weeks with diligent dressing changes. Severe (osteomyelitis) has guarded prognosis and may require surgery.
prevention: Solid floor (no wire), thick layer of soft hay/fleece bedding, keep bedding dry, maintain ideal body condition (BCS 5/9), avoid Rex breeding for show conformation.

name: Heat Stroke
cause: Rabbits, guinea pigs, and especially chinchillas cannot sweat and have a very narrow safe temperature range. Above 26 °C they begin to overheat; above 28 °C death can occur within hours. Chinchillas die above 25 °C reliably — their thick fur traps heat. Most cases occur during transport, in unventilated rooms, or when AC fails.
script: These animals don't sweat and they can't cool themselves like a dog can. Above 26 °C they overheat; chinchillas are even more sensitive — anything over 25 °C is dangerous. Most heat stroke cases happen during transport in a hot car, or when an AC fails on a hot day. Right now we're cooling slowly with cool water on the ears and IV fluids — cooling too fast can be just as dangerous as the overheating itself.
preventableNote: Almost entirely preventable with proper temperature control. Never transport in a closed hot car. Have a backup cooling plan if AC fails.
prognosis: Reversible if caught early. Once organ damage occurs (kidney failure, DIC), prognosis is guarded to grave.
prevention: AC or air-conditioned room with reliable backup. Frozen water bottles wrapped in cloth during hot days. Never transport in a closed car. Ceramic tile to lie on. Temp monitoring.

name: RHD (Rabbit Hemorrhagic Disease)
cause: A calicivirus (RHDV1 or RHDV2) that causes massive liver necrosis and hemorrhage. Highly contagious — transmitted by direct contact, but also via flies, fomites (shoes, clothes, hay), and even indirectly across cities. RHDV2 (newer strain, spreading globally since 2010) affects rabbits as young as 4 weeks; RHDV1 only affects adults. Death usually occurs within 1–3 days of exposure, often without warning signs.
script: RHD is a virus that destroys the liver within days. It's frighteningly contagious — even flies can carry it from an infected rabbit to a healthy one, and you can bring it home on your shoes. This is why even fully indoor rabbits need to be vaccinated. We can't cure it once symptoms start, but the vaccine is highly effective at preventing it.
preventableNote: Vaccination is highly effective. In countries where the vaccine isn't licensed (incl. parts of Asia/US), import via veterinary special access. The vaccine is the single most important preventive intervention for pet rabbits.
prognosis: Often fatal once symptomatic. Supportive care (fluids, plasma, vitamin K) sometimes helps but mortality > 80%.
prevention: Annual RHDV2 vaccination (Filavac VHD K C+V or Nobivac MYXO-RHD PLUS in combo regions). Quarantine new rabbits 30 days. Fly screens. Disinfect with 1% bleach (most disinfectants don't kill calicivirus).

name: Scurvy (Vitamin C Deficiency)
cause: Guinea pigs (and humans) lack the enzyme L-gulonolactone oxidase, so they cannot synthesize vitamin C. They must get it daily from food. Pellet-only diets, expired pellets, or rabbit pellets fed by mistake will cause deficiency within 2 weeks. Sick or pregnant pigs need 2–3× normal Vit C.
script: Guinea pigs are exactly like us — they can't make vitamin C; they must eat it every day. If the diet is short on fresh vegetables or the pellets are old, deficiency develops within two weeks. Once severe, the joints become painful, they bleed easily, and wounds won't heal. The good news is recovery is fast once we start supplementation — daily 30–50 mg/kg Vit C and a diet rich in bell peppers and dark leafy greens.
preventableNote: 100% preventable with proper daily Vit C — fresh vegetables + Vit-C-fortified pellets (replaced every 90 days as Vit C degrades).
prognosis: Excellent if caught early. Joint and dental damage may be permanent if chronic.
prevention: Daily 10–30 mg/kg dietary Vit C. Fresh bell pepper (highest natural source), parsley, dark leafy greens. Vit C pellets within 90 days of milling date. Liquid Vit C in water does NOT work (degrades in hours + bitter).

name: Ovarian Cysts
cause: Hormone-secreting cysts develop in the ovaries of 75% of intact female guinea pigs by age 3. They cause symmetric bilateral hair loss along the flanks, abdominal distension, behavior change, and sometimes pyometra. Driven by lifelong estrogen exposure — entirely a hormonal problem.
script: These cysts are extremely common in unspayed females — about 3 in 4 are affected by age 3. They're not cancer, but the hormones they make cause hair loss, behavioral changes, and pain. The only definitive treatment is spaying, which removes both ovaries and stops the hormone problem at the source.
preventableNote: Spaying at 4–6 months prevents it entirely. Once cysts develop, OVH is the cure.
prognosis: Excellent with OVH. Older sows with concurrent disease are higher surgical risk but still benefit.
prevention: Elective spay at 4–6 months. Abdominal ultrasound annually for intact sows over 18 months.

name: Dental Disease — Chinchilla (Slobbers)
cause: Same continuous-growth tooth biology as rabbits + GP, but chinchilla teeth grow even faster (3 mm/week) and the root reserves are very long, predisposing to root impaction into the eye socket or sinus. Predisposing factors: pellet-heavy diet (especially rabbit pellets, which are too high in Ca:P ratio), genetics, dropped from height.
script: Chinchilla teeth grow faster than any other pet — about 3 mm a week. They must chew very tough fibrous hay to wear them down. When the diet has too many pellets or the wrong type of pellet, the teeth grow uneven and form spurs, and the roots can grow upward into the eye socket. The drooling is from these sharp spurs cutting the tongue. We'll need to file them down today.
preventableNote: Diet-related ~60%; genetic predisposition ~40%. Diet changes help but cannot reverse genetic conformation.
prognosis: Manageable with periodic dental work. Once root impaction occurs (CT diagnosis), prognosis worsens.
prevention: Unlimited Timothy or Meadow hay. Use chinchilla-specific pellets (NOT rabbit). Avoid sugary treats. Dental + skull radiograph annually.

name: Wet Tail (Proliferative Ileitis)
cause: Caused by *Lawsonia intracellularis* (bacterium) in stressed weanling Syrian hamsters — typically just-rehomed pet store hamsters around 3–6 weeks old. Stress from transport, new environment, weaning, and overcrowding combines with the pathogen to cause severe watery diarrhea, dehydration, and death within 24–48h. Highly contagious between hamsters.
script: Wet tail is the most common emergency in young hamsters, especially ones just bought from a pet store. The stress of being weaned, packed in with other hamsters, and rehomed weakens their immune system, and a bacterium called Lawsonia takes hold. They get severe diarrhea and dehydrate very quickly. This is why we don't let young hamsters go home too early and why pet store hamsters often arrive already sick.
preventableNote: Largely a pet-store-hamster phenomenon. Buying older (8+ weeks), quarantining 14 days, and minimizing transport stress helps.
prognosis: Guarded — 50% mortality even with aggressive treatment. Survivors recover fully.
prevention: Buy from breeders not pet stores. Choose hamsters ≥ 8 weeks old. Quarantine 14 days. Stress-free environment. Don't mix new hamsters with existing ones.

name: Insulinoma
cause: A tumor of the insulin-producing beta cells of the pancreas. Extremely common in pet ferrets ≥ 3 years old — possibly the most common ferret cancer. Suspected causes: early neuter (US ferrets are neutered at 6 weeks), high-carbohydrate diets, and genetics. The tumor pours out insulin uncontrollably, dropping blood sugar to dangerous levels especially during fasting.
script: Insulinoma is a tumor on the pancreas that makes too much insulin, which crashes the blood sugar. It's extremely common in pet ferrets over 3 years old — we suspect it's linked to the early neutering most pet ferrets receive and the high-carb treats they often eat. The fasting that comes with the long sleep at night makes signs worst in the morning. We can manage it with frequent small meals + prednisone, but it can't be cured.
preventableNote: Cannot prevent the tumor once it develops. Reduce risk: avoid early neutering (some vets now wait until 6+ months), avoid sugary treats and high-carb foods.
prognosis: Manageable for 1–2 years with prednisone ± diazoxide + frequent meals. Surgical removal possible but tumors usually recur.
prevention: Avoid sugary treats (raisins, banana, fruit yogurt). Feed carnivore-appropriate diet only. Multiple small meals (q4–6h). Discuss timing of neuter with vet.

name: Adrenal Disease
cause: Hormone-secreting tumor (adenoma or carcinoma) of the adrenal cortex. Like insulinoma, suspected to be linked to early neutering — without sex hormone feedback from the gonads, the pituitary keeps signaling the adrenals, which eventually develop tumors. Causes hair loss (especially tail), vulvar swelling in spayed females, prostate enlargement and urinary obstruction in neutered males.
script: This is another disease linked to early neutering of pet ferrets. After we remove the ovaries or testicles young, the brain keeps signaling the adrenal glands to make more hormones, and over years a tumor develops. The tail hair loss, vulva swelling, and the smell are all from these hormones. The best treatment is either deslorelin implant or surgical removal of the affected adrenal.
preventableNote: Linked to early-neuter culture in pet ferrets. In some countries (UK), keeping ferrets intact and using deslorelin implants for behavior reduces incidence.
prognosis: Good with deslorelin implant (lasts ~12 months) or adrenalectomy. Right adrenal is hard to remove (cava). Median survival 2+ years with treatment.
prevention: Delay neutering until 6+ months if possible. Discuss deslorelin implant as alternative to surgical neuter.

name: Lymphoma — Ferret
cause: A cancer of lymphocytes that affects 25–30% of pet ferrets. Possibly virally induced (suspected retrovirus, not confirmed). Two forms: juvenile (aggressive, mediastinal mass, < 2 years) and adult (multicentric, > 4 years). Cause is not well understood — it's likely a combination of genetics, possibly a virus, and environmental factors.
script: Lymphoma is unfortunately one of the most common cancers in ferrets — about 1 in 4 will develop it. We suspect a virus may be involved but haven't proven it. There's nothing you did to cause this. Treatment depends on the form — the juvenile form is aggressive but the adult multicentric form often responds to chemotherapy with good quality of life for 1–2 years.
preventableNote: No known prevention.
prognosis: Juvenile mediastinal: poor (weeks–months). Adult multicentric: moderate with CHOP chemo (median 1–2 years).
prevention: No specific measures. Annual physical exam including LN palpation.

name: Wobbly Hedgehog Syndrome (WHS)
cause: A progressive degenerative neurological disease unique to African pygmy hedgehogs. Cause unknown but strongly genetic — affects 1 in 10 captive hedgehogs. Causes progressive paralysis starting in the hindlimbs, eventually affecting all four limbs. Onset usually at 18–24 months. There is NO TREATMENT and no cure.
script: Wobbly Hedgehog Syndrome is a progressive disease of the spinal cord that we believe is purely genetic — passed from parents. About 1 in 10 captive hedgehogs develop it, usually starting between 18 and 24 months of age. The wobbly gait will slowly worsen over 12–24 months. I'm sorry — there is no treatment. We can only make him comfortable, prevent secondary problems like pressure sores, and monitor for the point when quality of life is no longer acceptable.
preventableNote: Genetic — affected animals should not be bred. Buy from breeders who track WHS-free lines.
prognosis: Grave. Progressive paralysis over 12–24 months. Euthanasia eventually needed when unable to eat/eliminate normally.
prevention: Buy from reputable breeders who screen pedigrees for WHS. Affected hedgehogs and their parents should never be bred.

name: Hibernation Attempt
cause: African pygmy hedgehogs evolved in equatorial climates and have NOT developed true hibernation. When ambient temperature drops below 18 °C, their metabolism slows but cannot fully shut down — they enter a partial torpor that they cannot recover from on their own. Eventually fatal if not warmed slowly.
script: African hedgehogs come from warm climates — they can't actually hibernate properly. When their cage drops below 18 °C, they go into a half-asleep state called torpor that they can't wake up from on their own. This is a true emergency. We're going to warm her up slowly over 2–3 hours, give warm fluids, and watch for organ damage. Once she's recovered, you must make sure her cage temperature never drops below 24 °C — usually a ceramic heat emitter or heat lamp on a thermostat.
preventableNote: 100% preventable with proper heating. Most cases happen in cold seasons when owners forget to check enclosure temp.
prognosis: Good if found early and warmed gradually. Pneumonia and ketoacidosis are common complications.
prevention: Maintain enclosure at 24–28 °C year-round. Use a ceramic heat emitter on a thermostat. Backup heat source for winter power outages. Indoor digital thermometer in the cage.

name: Hindlimb Paralysis (HLP)
cause: Sugar gliders fed a calcium-deficient diet (especially fruit-heavy diets, "glider diets" of seeds/nuts, lack of insects with calcium dusting) develop low blood calcium. The parathyroid glands respond by pulling calcium out of bones, which weakens the spine and limbs. Eventually the spine fractures or the back legs simply stop working.
script: Hindlimb paralysis in sugar gliders is almost always caused by calcium deficiency from an imbalanced diet. If the diet is mostly fruit, or seeds and nuts, or commercial "glider food" without proper supplementation, the body pulls calcium from the bones until the spine becomes too weak to support the back legs. The good news is if we catch it early and correct the diet, we can reverse it. Long-term, the diet must change to a proper formula like Leadbeater's mix with calcium-dusted insects.
preventableNote: Almost 100% preventable with proper diet.
prognosis: Reversible if caught within 1–2 weeks with calcium injections + diet correction. Chronic cases have permanent neurological damage.
prevention: Proper diet: Leadbeater's mix OR HPW (high-protein Wombaroo) + calcium-dusted insects + small fruit portions. Ca:P ratio 2:1. UVB light helpful. Annual exam with blood calcium check.

name: Psittacosis (Chlamydia)
cause: Caused by *Chlamydia psittaci*, an intracellular bacterium. Birds catch it from other infected birds (often asymptomatic carriers — many pet store birds carry it). Stress reactivates latent infections. Transmitted via inhaled feather dust, dried droppings, and respiratory secretions. **ZOONOTIC** — humans can catch "parrot fever" (flu-like illness, occasionally severe pneumonia).
script: Psittacosis is caused by a bacterium called Chlamydia, which many pet store birds carry without showing signs. Stress — like the move to your home — can trigger active illness. It's contagious to humans too, so please wash hands carefully after handling and let me know if anyone at home develops flu-like symptoms. Treatment is a long 45-day course of doxycycline, which usually clears it completely.
preventableNote: Quarantine new birds 30–60 days + screen with PCR. Reduce stress (which triggers reactivation).
prognosis: Excellent response to 45-day doxycycline. Untreated cases can be fatal, especially in macaws and African greys.
prevention: PCR screening of all new birds. 60-day quarantine. Avoid stress. Owners should wash hands + avoid feather dust if immunocompromised.

name: PBFD (Psittacine Beak & Feather Disease)
cause: Caused by Beak & Feather Disease Virus (a circovirus). Highly contagious — transmitted by inhalation of feather dust, contact with infected birds, and via the egg. Cockatoos, lovebirds, eclectus, and African greys most susceptible. Most birds are infected as chicks. The virus destroys the feather follicles and beak — feathers fall out abnormally and the beak overgrows or becomes brittle. **No cure; eventual fatal.**
script: PBFD is a virus, similar to canine parvovirus in dogs. Once infected, there's no cure — the virus permanently damages the feathers and beak. Most birds get it as chicks, often from their parents at the breeder. It's heartbreaking but management focuses on quality of life and preventing spread to other birds. Cleanliness and isolation become very important.
preventableNote: PCR screening of breeding birds + chicks. Quarantine. Vaccine exists in Australia but not licensed in Asia/US.
prognosis: Grave long-term. Some birds live 1–3 years with good care; immunosuppression makes them prone to secondary infections.
prevention: PCR screen all new birds. 60-day quarantine. Disinfect with strong disinfectant (virus resists most). Affected birds isolated from healthy ones for life.

name: Avian Aspergillosis
cause: A fungal infection of the lungs and air sacs caused by *Aspergillus fumigatus* spores. The spores are EVERYWHERE in the environment — birds inhale them daily without issue when healthy. Disease develops when immunity drops: stress, poor diet (especially Vit A deficiency from seed-only diets), high spore load (mouldy peanuts, damp seed, dusty bedding), or other illness.
script: Aspergillus is a mould that's everywhere in the air — your bird breathes the spores every day without issue when healthy. It only causes disease when the bird is run-down: usually from a seed-only diet, mouldy food (peanuts are the biggest culprit), or another illness. Treatment is long (1–3 months of antifungal medication and nebulization) but very successful if started early.
preventableNote: Largely preventable: pellet-based diet, no peanuts, no mouldy food, dry well-ventilated cage.
prognosis: Good with prolonged antifungal therapy (voriconazole + nebulized amphotericin B). Late cases (granulomas filling lungs) have poor prognosis.
prevention: Pellet diet ≥ 60% (not seed). NO peanuts. Throw away food after 24h. Dry well-ventilated cage. Avoid damp wood substrate. F10 nebulization for high-risk birds.

name: Egg Binding (Dystocia) — Avian
cause: A retained egg in the oviduct that the hen cannot pass. Causes: calcium deficiency (the most common — egg shell needs calcium and so do the contractile muscles), obesity, lack of exercise, oversized egg (often from chronic egg-laying), inflammation in the oviduct, or environmental/temperature stress. **Common in budgies, cockatiels, lovebirds, and finches kept in single-bird homes that lay infertile eggs repeatedly.**
script: Egg-binding is when she can't pass the egg out of her body. The most common cause is calcium deficiency — the muscles of the oviduct need calcium to contract properly. A seed-only diet, lack of UV light, or laying eggs too frequently all deplete her calcium stores. We need to act quickly because the egg can compress nerves and blood vessels and become an emergency.
preventableNote: Diet + UV + limiting hormonal triggers (no mirror, no nest box, < 10h light/day) can reduce frequency. Once chronic layer, hormone implants help.
prognosis: Good if treated within 24h. Past 48h, egg necrosis and septicemia.
prevention: Pellet diet + dark leafy greens. UVB light. Limit photoperiod < 10h/day. NO mirrors or nest boxes for single hens. Deslorelin implant for chronic layers.

name: Heavy Metal Toxicity
cause: Birds chew constantly. Metal toys, galvanized cage wire (zinc), old paint chips (lead), curtain weights, costume jewelry, lead-soldered stained glass, and even some commercial bird toys can release toxic metals into the digestive tract. Causes neurological signs (head tilt, seizures), GI signs (vomiting, regurgitation), and anemia.
script: Birds love to chew, and unfortunately metals like zinc and lead are everywhere in homes — old paint, curtain weights, even some cage wire and toys. When swallowed, these metals are absorbed and damage the nervous system and red blood cells. We'll need radiographs to see if there's still metal in the gut and start chelation therapy with CaEDTA. We need to find what she chewed and remove it from her environment.
preventableNote: 100% preventable by bird-proofing the environment.
prognosis: Good if treated early. Severe neurological signs may leave permanent damage.
prevention: Stainless steel cage (no galvanized). Bird-safe toys only — avoid bells with lead clappers. No painted antique furniture access. Test old painted surfaces. Remove all small metal items.

name: Teflon Toxicity (PTFE Fume Fever)
cause: PTFE (Teflon, non-stick coating) releases lethal fumes when heated above 280 °C. Birds have extremely efficient respiratory systems and die within minutes of exposure. Sources: non-stick cookware overheated empty, self-cleaning ovens, some heat lamps, irons, hair dryers, and rice cookers with non-stick coatings.
script: Teflon — the non-stick coating on pans — releases an invisible toxic fume when heated above 280 °C, like an empty pan on the stove or a self-cleaning oven cycle. Birds breathe so efficiently that even one exposure can be fatal within minutes. There is no antidote — we can only provide oxygen and supportive care. Going forward, NO non-stick cookware in the home, ever. Switch to stainless steel or cast iron.
preventableNote: 100% preventable by removing all PTFE from the home.
prognosis: Grave once symptomatic. Death usually within 30 min of exposure.
prevention: Remove ALL non-stick cookware (Teflon, ceramic with PTFE backing, non-stick rice cookers). Use stainless steel, cast iron, or ceramic. No self-cleaning oven. Check heat lamp specs.

name: Hypocalcemia — African Grey
cause: African grey parrots specifically have unique calcium metabolism — they cannot mobilize calcium from bone as effectively as other birds. When on a seed-only diet, with no UV light, and no calcium supplement, blood calcium drops dramatically. They present with full-body seizures, often suddenly in a previously "healthy"-looking 3–5-year-old bird.
script: African greys are unusually vulnerable to calcium deficiency — even more than other parrots. A seed-based diet without UV light or calcium supplementation will eventually cause low blood calcium, and the brain becomes hyperexcitable until it seizes. The seizures are dramatic but usually fully reversible once we give IV calcium. Long-term, the diet must be pellet-based plus dark leafy greens, with UV light during the day.
preventableNote: 100% preventable with proper diet + UV.
prognosis: Excellent immediate response to IV calcium gluconate. Long-term: diet correction prevents recurrence.
prevention: Pellet diet ≥ 70% (Harrison's, Roudybush, Zupreem). Daily fresh dark leafy greens. Calcium-rich foods (almonds with shell). UVB-emitting bulb 10–12h/day. Annual ionized calcium check.

name: Metabolic Bone Disease (MBD)
cause: Reptiles need UVB light to make vitamin D, which is needed to absorb calcium from food. Without UVB (no bulb, expired bulb, bulb behind glass), or without dietary calcium (insect-only diet without dusting), they develop low blood calcium. The body pulls calcium from bones, causing soft jaw, "rubber jaw" appearance, spontaneous limb fractures, and eventually seizures.
script: MBD is the single most common nutritional problem we see in reptiles. To absorb calcium from food, reptiles need UVB light — and the bulb has to be a strong reptile UVB bulb that's replaced every 6 months (the UV runs out even when the light still works). They also need calcium dusted on their insects every meal. When either is missing, the body slowly takes calcium from the bones until they become so soft that limbs fracture spontaneously. The damage we already see is permanent, but we can stop progression.
preventableNote: Almost 100% preventable with proper UVB + calcium supplementation.
prognosis: Mild cases reverse fully with treatment. Severe cases have permanent skeletal deformity. Spinal involvement = permanent paralysis.
prevention: Strong reptile UVB bulb (10.0–12.0), full tube length, replaced q6 months. Calcium powder dusted on insects every meal (juveniles) or every other meal (adults). UVB cannot pass through glass — bulb inside enclosure. Annual blood calcium + UV meter check.

name: Mouth Rot (Infectious Stomatitis)
cause: A bacterial infection (sometimes secondary to fungi or virus) of the oral cavity. Almost always secondary to poor husbandry: temperatures below POTZ (immunity drops), stress (overcrowding, handling, prey injury), trauma to mouth, or systemic illness. Common bacteria: Pseudomonas, Aeromonas, Salmonella.
script: Mouth rot starts as a bacterial infection of the gums and mouth lining. It almost always happens when the reptile is stressed or kept cooler than its preferred temperature — both weaken the immune system and let the normal mouth bacteria overgrow. We'll culture and start antibiotics, but the real fix is correcting the enclosure temperature, reducing stress, and improving overall husbandry.
preventableNote: Largely preventable by maintaining POTZ + good husbandry.
prognosis: Good with antibiotics + husbandry correction. Severe cases progress to osteomyelitis of the jaw — guarded prognosis.
prevention: Maintain POTZ (see Reptile POTZ Reference). Reduce handling stress. Inspect mouth monthly. Treat any cut or abrasion immediately. Pre-killed prey only (live prey can injure mouth).

name: Reptile Respiratory Infection (RI)
cause: Bacterial (most commonly) or fungal infection of the lungs. Almost always secondary to poor husbandry: low temperature (esp. at night), high humidity in a desert species (or vice versa), inadequate ventilation, or chronic stress. Symptoms: open-mouth breathing, mucus around nose, audible respiration, lethargy.
script: Respiratory infections in reptiles are almost always a husbandry problem. Too cold, especially at night, drops their immune system; too damp or too dry for the species lets bacteria thrive. We'll start antibiotics and review the enclosure — temperature, humidity, ventilation — because antibiotics alone won't cure it if the underlying cause stays the same.
preventableNote: Largely preventable with correct POTZ + species-appropriate humidity.
prognosis: Good early. Severe pneumonia with hypoxia has guarded prognosis. May need air sac cannula for ventilation in lizards/snakes.
prevention: POTZ correct day + night. Species-appropriate humidity (desert species 30–40%, tropical 60–80%). Good ventilation. Quarantine new reptiles 90 days. Annual physical exam.

name: Reptile Egg Binding (Dystocia)
cause: Female reptile cannot pass her eggs. Causes: low calcium (most common, see MBD), incorrect nesting site (she won't lay without a proper nesting box), dehydration, oversize egg, infected oviduct, or hormonal imbalance. Common in green iguanas, bearded dragons, and chelonians — especially in animals that have laid eggs successfully before but suddenly can't.
script: Egg-binding has many possible causes, but in reptiles the most common is calcium deficiency — the muscles of the oviduct need calcium to contract. Another common reason is the absence of a proper nest site — many females will hold onto eggs rather than lay them in the wrong place. Today we'll give calcium IV, fluids, and a warm nesting box. If she still can't lay in 24 hours, we may need surgery.
preventableNote: Correct husbandry (UVB, calcium, proper nest box) prevents most cases. Some are unavoidable (oviduct disease, malformed eggs).
prognosis: Good with prompt treatment. Past 72h, oviduct rupture or infection.
prevention: UVB + calcium. Always provide a nest box for sexually mature females, regardless of presence of a male. Annual reproductive exam.

name: Hypovitaminosis A — Chelonian
cause: Iceberg lettuce, cucumber, and other low-Vit-A vegetables fed exclusively to turtles and tortoises cause vitamin A deficiency. Box turtles fed dog/cat food (which has Vit A but disrupts the calcium balance) are also at risk. Vit A deficiency causes swollen eyes, respiratory infection, aural abscesses (box turtles), and squamous metaplasia of the kidneys.
script: Most cases of swollen eyes, runny nose, and ear abscesses in turtles and tortoises come from vitamin A deficiency. This happens when the diet is too narrow — iceberg lettuce, cucumber, and the like don't have enough vitamin A. We need to inject vitamin A today and shift the diet to a wider variety of dark leafy greens, yellow vegetables, and occasional egg yolk for omnivores.
preventableNote: 100% preventable with proper diet.
prognosis: Good with vitamin A supplementation + diet correction. Aural abscess often needs surgical lancing.
prevention: Varied diet: dark leafy greens (dandelion, mulberry, plantain), yellow vegetables (squash, sweet potato). Omnivores can have small egg yolk weekly. Avoid iceberg lettuce + cucumber as primary diet. NO dog/cat food.

name: Shell Rot (Ulcerative Shell Disease)
cause: Bacterial or fungal infection of the shell. In aquatic turtles, almost always from poor water quality — high ammonia, low pH, inadequate filtration, no dry basking area. In land tortoises, from damp substrate or trauma. Once the shell scute is breached, bacteria invade the bone underneath.
script: Shell rot is an infection of the shell, almost always from a husbandry problem. For your aquatic turtle, the water quality is the main thing — ammonia and bacteria build up, and once a small abrasion forms on the shell, infection sets in. We need to address the cause (better filtration, dry basking spot, partial water changes) at the same time we treat the infection.
preventableNote: Almost entirely preventable with proper husbandry.
prognosis: Good if treated before the bone is involved. Deep shell rot with osteomyelitis is guarded.
prevention: Aquatic: weekly partial water change, strong filter, dry basking spot ≥ daily, UVB. Land: dry substrate (no damp moss in cool zones), inspect shell weekly.

name: Chytridiomycosis
cause: Caused by *Batrachochytrium dendrobatidis* (Bd), a fungus that infects the keratinized skin of amphibians. Transmitted through water and direct contact between amphibians. Most pet amphibians caught wild-caught or sourced from infected colonies are exposed. The fungus disrupts skin function — amphibians breathe and absorb electrolytes through skin — and death is from electrolyte imbalance.
script: Chytrid is a fungus that has caused mass extinctions in wild frog populations worldwide. It attacks the skin, which amphibians use for breathing and water balance. The good news is most pet amphibians can be cured with a course of antifungal baths if caught early. Going forward, anything new entering the enclosure — new frog, plant, water — should be quarantined and ideally screened with PCR.
preventableNote: Quarantine + PCR testing of new amphibians. Source from chytrid-free breeders.
prognosis: Good with itraconazole baths (0.01% × 5–11 min daily × 11 days). Late cases die from electrolyte imbalance.
prevention: PCR screen new amphibians. 60-day quarantine in separate room with separate equipment. Disinfect tools with 1% bleach. Don't mix wild-caught + captive.

name: Canine Parvovirus
cause: A highly contagious virus (CPV-2 strain) that attacks rapidly dividing cells — intestinal lining and bone marrow. Spread by direct contact with infected faeces, contaminated surfaces (shoes, clothes, ground), and the virus survives in the environment for **months to a year**. Unvaccinated puppies 6–20 weeks are highest risk. The waning of maternal antibody at this age is why we vaccinate at 8, 12, 16 weeks.
script: Parvo is a virus that destroys the lining of the intestines and the bone marrow. It's extremely contagious — your puppy could have caught it from any surface where an infected dog had been months ago, even from soil on your shoes. This is why we vaccinate puppies three times — between 6 and 16 weeks the protection from their mother fades, and we need to build their own immunity before they're exposed. Treatment is intensive supportive care for 5–7 days, and most puppies recover if we can support them through the worst of it.
preventableNote: Vaccination is highly effective. Do not take unvaccinated puppies to public dog areas until 2 weeks after the final puppy shot.
prognosis: 70–90% survival with intensive hospitalization. Without treatment, 80%+ mortality.
prevention: Vaccinate at 8, 12, 16 weeks (some protocols add 20 weeks for high-risk breeds — Rottweilers, Dobermans). Annual booster. Don't walk unvaccinated puppies in public areas.

name: Canine Distemper
cause: A morbillivirus (relative of measles) that affects respiratory, GI, and neurological systems. Transmitted by aerosol from infected dogs/wildlife. Vaccine-preventable in dogs. **Always fatal in unvaccinated ferrets and many exotic carnivores** — which is why the vaccine must be a recombinant canarypox type (PUREVAX), NOT modified-live, in those species.
script: Distemper is a viral disease that affects the lungs, gut, and eventually the brain. It's preventable by vaccine — your dog's puppy shots and yearly booster protect against it. The reason we keep stressing the booster is that the brain damage from distemper is permanent and irreversible. If you ever bring a new puppy home, make sure it's vaccinated before mixing with your dog.
preventableNote: Vaccine is highly effective. Combined with parvo, adeno, parainfluenza (DHPP/DAPP).
prognosis: High mortality in unvaccinated dogs. Survivors often have permanent neurological signs (myoclonus, seizures, hard pad).
prevention: DHPP/DAPP vaccine at 8, 12, 16 weeks, then 1 year, then q1–3y. For exotic carnivores (ferret, fennec, meerkat): PUREVAX recombinant only.

name: Heartworm Disease
cause: *Dirofilaria immitis*, a parasitic worm transmitted by mosquitoes. Larvae from a mosquito bite migrate over months to the heart and pulmonary arteries, where adult worms (up to 30 cm long) cause coughing, exercise intolerance, eventually right-sided heart failure. **Ferrets are extremely susceptible — even one worm can be fatal because their heart is so small.**
script: Heartworm comes from mosquito bites. The larvae take about 6 months to grow into adult worms that live in the heart and lung blood vessels, where they cause coughing and eventually heart failure. The treatment for established infection is harsh and risky for the dog — which is why monthly prevention is so much better than treatment. Ferrets are even more sensitive, which is why monthly prevention is critical for them too.
preventableNote: Monthly oral or topical preventive (ivermectin, selamectin, moxidectin) is highly effective.
prognosis: Treatment with melarsomine is risky (anaphylaxis, embolism). Prevention is dramatically safer and cheaper.
prevention: Monthly preventive year-round (Heartgard, Revolution, Advocate, Sentinel). Annual snap test. Mosquito control around home.

name: Feline Lower Urinary Tract Disease (FLUTD)
cause: A syndrome of bladder inflammation in cats. The single biggest cause is **stress** — multi-cat households, change of routine, new pet, owner away, etc. Diet (dry food only, low water intake) and obesity also contribute. About 60% are "idiopathic" (FIC — no infection, just stress-induced inflammation). 20% are bladder stones (struvite, calcium oxalate). 10% urethral plugs. Less than 5% are true bacterial UTI.
script: In cats, "urinary problems" are almost always stress-related — not infection like we see in dogs. The bladder lining gets inflamed from stress hormones, which causes the pain and bloody urine. Things like a new pet, you working late, even rearranging furniture can trigger it. We'll start pain meds and fluids, but the long-term fix is reducing stress and increasing water intake — wet food, water fountains, more litterboxes, vertical space, environmental enrichment.
preventableNote: Stress management + wet food + environmental enrichment significantly reduces episodes.
prognosis: Episodes resolve in 5–7 days. Recurrence is common (50% within a year). Male cats can develop life-threatening urethral obstruction.
prevention: Wet food ≥ 50% of diet. Water fountains. 1 litterbox per cat + 1. Feliway diffuser. Vertical space. Predictable routine. **MALE CATS** with FLUTD episode: urgent re-exam if straining without urine = potential obstruction = EMERGENCY.

name: Chronic Kidney Disease (Cat CKD)
cause: Progressive loss of kidney function — irreversible. Affects 1 in 3 cats over age 12. Multiple causes contributing over a lifetime: dehydration from dry-food diets, chronic high blood pressure, previous urinary infections, dental disease (bacteremia damages kidneys), high-phosphorus diet, age. There's rarely a single cause we can point to.
script: Kidney disease in older cats is extremely common — about 1 in 3 cats over 12 have it. There's rarely a single thing that caused it — it's a combination of years of slight dehydration (cats on dry food only chronically take in less water than they need), age, dental disease, and other factors. We can't reverse the damage, but with the right diet, fluids, and monitoring, many cats live comfortably for years after diagnosis.
preventableNote: Risk reduction: wet food, water fountains, regular dental care, annual senior bloodwork from age 7.
prognosis: Years of good quality life with proper management. Stage 4 (uremic) has poor prognosis.
prevention: Wet food ≥ 50% of diet. Annual SDMA + UPC from age 7. Prescription renal diet once diagnosed. Sub-Q fluids at home when indicated.

name: Feline Hyperthyroidism
cause: A benign tumor (adenoma) of the thyroid gland that overproduces thyroid hormone. Affects 10% of cats over 10 years old. Cause is suspected to involve environmental factors (flame-retardant chemicals in furniture and dust, BPA in canned food lining, iodine fluctuation in fish-based diets), genetic predisposition, and aging.
script: Hyperthyroidism is a benign tumor on the thyroid gland that makes too much thyroid hormone — which speeds up everything in the body. That's why she's losing weight despite eating well, vocalizing more, and seems restless. The cause isn't fully understood — we suspect a mix of environmental chemicals, genetics, and aging. The good news is it's very treatable — radioactive iodine treatment is curative; methimazole tablets, transdermal cream, or prescription diet are also effective.
preventableNote: No known prevention. Reduce exposure to environmental endocrine disruptors theoretically helpful but unproven.
prognosis: Excellent with treatment. Radioactive iodine is curative. Methimazole controls signs for years.
prevention: Annual T4 from age 7. Wet pouches (avoid canned BPA-lined). Stainless steel/ceramic bowls (avoid plastic).

name: Diabetes Mellitus
cause: In dogs: type I (insulin-deficient) — usually autoimmune destruction of pancreatic beta cells, with genetic predisposition (Schnauzer, Samoyed, Bichon). In cats: type II (insulin-resistant) — driven by obesity + high-carb diet + age + genetic predisposition. About 80% of feline DM is reversible if caught early and the cat is switched to a low-carb wet diet with prompt insulin therapy.
script: In cats, diabetes is almost always linked to obesity and dry-food diets — too many carbohydrates and not enough exercise overwhelm the pancreas. The good news is that about 4 out of 5 newly-diagnosed cats can be put into remission — meaning come off insulin entirely — if we catch it early, switch to a low-carb wet food diet, and start insulin promptly. In dogs, it's genetic and they'll need insulin lifelong, but we can manage it well.
preventableNote: Cat: 70% reduction with lean body weight + wet food. Dog: not preventable.
prognosis: Cat: 80% remission rate with early intervention + low-carb diet. Dog: lifelong management, good with good owner compliance.
prevention: Cat: maintain lean BCS, wet food ≥ 50%, exercise. Dog: annual fasting glucose in predisposed breeds from age 5.

name: Otitis Externa
cause: Inflammation of the outer ear canal. The most common cause is **allergies** (food allergy or environmental atopy) — the inflammation creates a moist warm environment that yeast (Malassezia) and bacteria (Staphylococcus, Pseudomonas) then overgrow in. Other contributors: ear conformation (Cocker Spaniel, Basset Hound — heavy floppy ears), excess moisture (swimming), foreign body (grass awn), ear mites (Otodectes), tumor.
script: Ear infections in dogs are almost always a sign of an underlying allergy — the allergy causes inflammation in the ear, which lets yeast and bacteria overgrow. So while we'll clean and treat the infection today, we usually need to look at what's triggering the allergy too — often food, sometimes environmental (pollen, dust mites). Otherwise the infections come back every few months.
preventableNote: Address underlying allergy. Routine ear cleaning in predisposed breeds.
prognosis: Good for acute episode. Recurrence common without allergy management.
prevention: Weekly ear cleaner (chlorhexidine/Tris-EDTA) in predisposed breeds. Dry ears thoroughly after swimming. Hypoallergenic diet trial if recurring. Allergy testing for chronic cases.

name: Intervertebral Disc Disease (IVDD)
cause: The cushioning disc between vertebrae degenerates (Hansen type I — sudden, in chondrodystrophic breeds like Dachshund, Corgi, Shih Tzu) or slowly bulges (Hansen type II — older large breeds). When the disc material pushes on the spinal cord, signs range from pain to total paralysis. Genetic in chondrodystrophic breeds — the cartilage degenerates abnormally from a young age.
script: In dachshunds and other "long-back" breeds, the discs between the vertebrae are genetically prone to degenerating early and slipping. When the slipped disc material pushes on the spinal cord, it causes the pain and weakness you're seeing. Time matters a lot here — the longer the cord is compressed, the less likely full recovery becomes. We need to do an MRI today to decide between strict rest with pain meds vs. surgical decompression.
preventableNote: Genetic in chondrodystrophic breeds — cannot prevent the predisposition. Risk reduction: lean body weight, ramps not stairs/jumps, harness not neck collar.
prognosis: Mild (pain only) — 90%+ recover with rest. Severe (paralysis but deep pain present) — 90%+ recover with surgery within 24h. Loss of deep pain > 24h — guarded.
prevention: Maintain ideal weight in at-risk breeds. Discourage jumping on/off furniture. Ramps for cars and beds. Harness not neck collar. Consider genetic testing (CDDY) before breeding.

name: Mammary Tumors
cause: Hormonal — ovarian estrogen and progesterone drive mammary cell proliferation. Risk is dramatically reduced by spaying before first heat. In dogs, 50% of mammary tumors are malignant. In cats, **80% are malignant** and aggressive. Risk in unspayed dogs is 26% lifetime; in dogs spayed before first heat it's 0.5%.
script: Mammary tumors are driven by hormones from the ovaries. The dramatic risk reduction from spaying before the first heat is one of the strongest preventive effects we know of — from 26% lifetime risk in intact females to under 1% if spayed before 6 months. In cats, these tumors are usually aggressive — 4 out of 5 are cancer that can spread. We'll need biopsy and likely surgical removal of the chain.
preventableNote: Spay before first heat = ~99% risk reduction in dogs. Spay before age 1 = 86% reduction in cats.
prognosis: Dog: 50% benign, 50% malignant. Cat: 80% malignant — chain mastectomy + chemotherapy. Prognosis depends on size, grade, lymph node involvement.
prevention: Spay between 4–6 months. Monthly mammary palpation in older intact bitches. Annual exam.

name: Cushing's Disease (HAC)
cause: Overproduction of cortisol. Two main causes: (1) **Pituitary-dependent (PDH)** — 85% of cases — a small benign tumor in the pituitary gland signals the adrenals to overproduce. (2) **Adrenal-dependent (ADH)** — 15% — a tumor on the adrenal gland itself. Iatrogenic Cushing's (from long-term steroid use) is also common. Genetic predisposition: Poodle, Dachshund, Boxer, Beagle.
script: Cushing's is when the body makes too much cortisol — the "stress hormone." About 85% of the time it's caused by a small benign tumor in the pituitary gland that signals the adrenals to overproduce. The pot-belly, increased drinking, hair loss, and panting are all from chronic high cortisol. We can't cure it but we can control it well with medication, and most dogs live a normal lifespan.
preventableNote: No known prevention except avoiding chronic high-dose steroid therapy (causes iatrogenic Cushing's).
prognosis: Good with trilostane or mitotane therapy. Median 2–3 years post-diagnosis (often die of other age-related disease).
prevention: No specific prevention. Avoid prolonged steroid courses where alternatives exist.

name: FeLV (Feline Leukemia Virus)
cause: A retrovirus transmitted between cats through prolonged close contact — mutual grooming, shared food/water bowls, bite wounds, mother-to-kitten in utero or via milk. Outdoor unvaccinated cats are at highest risk. Kittens are far more susceptible than adults — most adult cats clear the virus, while kittens often become persistently infected.
script: FeLV is a virus that spreads between cats through close contact like grooming or shared bowls. Kittens are much more susceptible than adults — once they're persistently infected, the virus suppresses their immune system over time. We can't cure it, but with careful management, indoor lifestyle, and aggressive treatment of any infections, FeLV-positive cats can have years of good quality life. We should also test any other cats in the household and vaccinate at-risk kittens.
preventableNote: Vaccine highly effective. Indoor lifestyle eliminates exposure risk. Test before introducing new cats.
prognosis: Variable — some persistently infected cats live 5+ years, others develop lymphoma or anemia within 2–3 years.
prevention: Test all new cats before introduction. Vaccinate at-risk kittens (8 weeks + booster). Keep indoor. Don't mix FeLV+ with FeLV− cats.

name: FIP (Feline Infectious Peritonitis)
cause: Caused by a mutation of feline coronavirus (FCoV). FCoV itself is extremely common in multi-cat homes/catteries — most cats are exposed and shed it harmlessly in faeces. In a small percentage (5–10%), the virus mutates inside the cat's body into a deadly form that causes FIP. Stress, young age (most cases 6 months – 2 years), and immune-incompetent breeds (Bengal, Birman, Persian) increase risk.
script: FIP is one of the most heartbreaking diseases in cats. It comes from a common virus that almost all cats in multi-cat households catch as kittens — usually it causes nothing or mild diarrhea. But in about 5–10% of cats, the virus mutates inside their own body into the deadly form. This happens months after the original infection, often triggered by stress like rehoming. The good news is that newer antiviral treatments (GS-441524) can cure most cases if started early — we should discuss this option.
preventableNote: Reduce risk: low-stress kitten rearing, smaller groups, good hygiene. No reliable vaccine.
prognosis: Historically fatal. With GS-441524 antiviral (84-day course), 85%+ cure rate.
prevention: Reduce stress at adoption. Quarantine new cats. Don't over-house catteries. Test queens. Consider antiviral for high-risk kittens.

name: Pyometra
cause: Bacterial infection (often *E. coli*) of the uterus, occurring 4–8 weeks after a heat cycle. The progesterone-primed uterine lining becomes susceptible to ascending infection from the vagina. Affects intact females, especially over 6 years old. The uterus fills with pus and bacterial toxins enter the bloodstream — septic, life-threatening within days.
script: Pyometra is a severe uterine infection that happens to unspayed females, usually a few weeks after a heat cycle. The hormone progesterone primes the uterine lining to be vulnerable to bacteria from the normal vaginal flora. The uterus fills with pus and the toxins poison the whole body. We need emergency surgery to remove the uterus today. This is exactly why we recommend spaying — pyometra cannot happen in a spayed female.
preventableNote: 100% preventable by spaying.
prognosis: Good with emergency OVH + IV fluids + antibiotics if uterus hasn't ruptured. Rupture → septic peritonitis, guarded.
prevention: Spay before age 5 ideally. Monitor older intact females for vulvar discharge 4–8 weeks post-heat.

name: Cryptosporidiosis
cause: Caused by *Cryptosporidium* protozoan parasites (Cryptosporidium serpentis in snakes, C. parvum and C. galli in birds, C. saurophilum in bearded dragons). Transmitted by ingestion of oocysts in contaminated food, water, or substrate. Highly contagious between animals, very resistant to disinfectants. Once an enclosure is infected, decontamination is extremely difficult. In snakes especially, currently considered **not curable** — only managed.
script: Cryptosporidium is a parasite that's notoriously hard to treat — especially in snakes, where we currently have no cure, only management. Once an enclosure has it, the parasites form spores that survive nearly all disinfectants. We'll start aggressive supportive care and a long course of paromomycin, but I have to be honest about the prognosis. We'll also need to disinfect everything with steam or ammonia, and isolate this animal from any other reptiles indefinitely.
preventableNote: Quarantine all new reptiles 90 days + fecal screen. Source from screened breeders.
prognosis: Snake: grave (no cure currently). Bearded dragon: guarded with paromomycin + nitazoxanide. Bird: variable.
prevention: 90-day quarantine + multiple negative fecals before introduction. Don't share enclosure items. Use ammonia disinfection (chlorhexidine + bleach don't kill oocysts).

name: Atherosclerosis
cause: Buildup of cholesterol and fat in the arteries, especially common in older parrots fed seed-only or high-fat diets. African greys, Amazons, and Quaker parrots are most predisposed. Decades of seed-based diet + sedentary lifestyle in a cage = arterial disease similar to humans. Often goes undetected until sudden collapse from a heart attack or stroke.
script: Atherosclerosis is the same disease people get — fat builds up in the arteries over years until the heart can't keep up or has a heart attack. Older parrots on seed-based diets are especially affected, and African greys and Amazons are particularly prone genetically. We can't reverse the existing damage, but we can slow progression with a pellet-based low-fat diet, more exercise out of the cage, and medications if echocardiogram shows heart involvement.
preventableNote: Pellet diet + exercise from young age significantly reduces risk.
prognosis: Manageable for years with diet + exercise. Sudden death possible from acute event.
prevention: Pellet diet ≥ 70% from young age. Daily out-of-cage flight time. Limit fatty seeds (especially sunflower, peanuts) to occasional treats. Annual echo from age 10 in predisposed species.

name: Macrorhabdus (Megabacteria)
cause: *Macrorhabdus ornithogaster* is actually a yeast (not bacteria, despite the historical name) that infects the proventriculus (glandular stomach) of small birds. Common in budgies, cockatiels, and finches. Transmitted bird-to-bird via faeces; spreads in aviaries quickly. Causes chronic weight loss, undigested seed in faeces, vomiting, and "going light" syndrome. Stress allows the latent infection to become symptomatic.
script: Megabacteria is actually a yeast — the name is from when we first discovered it and thought it was a bacterium. It lives in the stomach of small birds, especially budgies and cockatiels. Many birds carry it silently and only get sick when stressed. The weight loss and undigested seed in the droppings are classic. Treatment is amphotericin B by mouth for 30 days — it works well if we catch it early.
preventableNote: Quarantine + faecal screening of new birds. Reduce flock stress.
prognosis: Good with amphotericin B PO × 30 days. Chronic cases with severe weight loss have guarded prognosis.
prevention: 30-day quarantine of all new birds with faecal wet mount. Separate sick birds. Disinfect cages between birds.

name: Chocolate Toxicity
cause: Theobromine and caffeine in chocolate cannot be efficiently metabolized by dogs (or cats, ferrets). Toxic dose depends on type of chocolate: dark/baking chocolate is 4–8× more dangerous than milk chocolate. White chocolate has very little theobromine. Symptoms: vomiting, hyperactivity, tachycardia, tremors, seizures, death. Toxic dose: ~20 mg/kg theobromine causes signs; 60 mg/kg can be fatal.
script: Chocolate contains theobromine, which dogs can't metabolize well — it builds up to toxic levels. The darker the chocolate, the more theobromine: a small piece of dark chocolate is much more dangerous than a piece of milk chocolate. We need to know roughly how much and what kind to calculate the risk. If it was recent (under 2 hours), we can induce vomiting; otherwise we treat symptoms as they develop. Going forward, please keep all chocolate out of reach — dogs are very food-motivated and will find it.
preventableNote: 100% preventable by keeping chocolate inaccessible.
prognosis: Excellent if treated early. Severe cases (seizure, arrhythmia) have guarded prognosis.
prevention: Store all chocolate in closed cabinets. Educate household and visiting children. Never feed as treat. Sugar-free gum (xylitol) is even more dangerous — see Xylitol Toxicity.

name: Xylitol Toxicity
cause: Xylitol (an artificial sweetener in sugar-free gum, candy, peanut butter, baked goods, some toothpaste) causes massive insulin release in dogs and ferrets, dropping blood sugar to dangerously low levels within 30–60 minutes. Higher doses (≥ 500 mg/kg) cause acute liver failure 12–72 hours later. **Cats and humans are NOT affected** — only dogs and ferrets.
script: Xylitol is found in many sugar-free products — gum, candy, certain peanut butters, even some baked goods. Dogs treat it like sugar and dump out massive amounts of insulin, which crashes their blood sugar within an hour. At higher doses it also causes liver failure 1–3 days later. This is actually more dangerous than chocolate in many cases. Please check ingredient labels carefully — sugar-free gum in a handbag has killed dogs.
preventableNote: 100% preventable with awareness. Check ingredients on peanut butter especially.
prognosis: Hypoglycemia phase: excellent with IV dextrose. Liver failure phase: guarded.
prevention: Audit home for xylitol-containing products. Check peanut butter labels (some brands use it). Never give human sugar-free anything. Keep handbags out of reach.

name: Lily Toxicity
cause: **True lilies (Lilium species) and daylilies (Hemerocallis) cause acute kidney failure in cats**, even from minimal exposure — biting a leaf, drinking water from the vase, or licking pollen off fur. The toxic compound is unidentified. All parts of the plant are toxic. Even a small ingestion can be fatal without aggressive treatment.
script: Lilies are extraordinarily toxic to cats — even brushing pollen off her fur and grooming it off can cause kidney failure. The toxic compound isn't fully understood but it specifically targets cat kidneys. Other species (dogs, humans) aren't affected, which is why people don't realize the danger. We need to start aggressive IV fluids right now to try to protect the kidneys, and going forward please remove all lilies from the home permanently.
preventableNote: 100% preventable by removing lilies from cat's environment.
prognosis: Treated within 6h: good. Past 18h: guarded. Past 36h: grave (irreversible renal failure).
prevention: NO lilies in homes with cats — including cut flower arrangements. Educate florists. Many "Easter" / "Tiger" / "Asiatic" / "Stargazer" lilies all toxic.

name: Ferret GI Foreign Body
cause: Ferrets chew everything. Rubber items (erasers, shoe soles, foam, rubber bands, headphone cushions), small toys, and hair ties are the most common items swallowed. The narrow ferret intestine cannot pass these, causing obstruction. Especially common in ferrets under 2 years old. Trichobezoars (hairballs) also common in older ferrets during shed season.
script: Ferrets are notorious for chewing and swallowing rubber. Anything soft and squishy seems to attract them — eraser, shoe sole, foam, headphone cushions, rubber bands. The intestine is too narrow to pass these objects, so they cause obstruction. Today's radiographs show an object in the small intestine — we need surgery now. Going forward, ferret-proofing is critical: nothing rubber, foam, or small enough to swallow within reach when she's out of the cage.
preventableNote: Ferret-proof environment + supervise out-of-cage time.
prognosis: Excellent with prompt surgery. Delay > 24h → bowel necrosis, perforation, peritonitis (grave).
prevention: Remove all rubber/foam objects from rooms where ferret has access. Supervise out-of-cage time. Lurp inspection of all toys monthly.

name: Liver Lobe Torsion
cause: A liver lobe (most commonly the caudate lobe) twists on its blood supply, cutting off circulation and causing necrosis. The exact trigger is unknown — proposed mechanisms include congenital ligamentous laxity, trauma from a fall or rough handling, or sudden movement after a period of inactivity. More common in middle-aged to older lop-eared rabbits but seen in all breeds. Often misdiagnosed as GI stasis because the signs overlap — anorexia, hunched posture, abdominal pain.
script: Liver lobe torsion is when one of the lobes of the liver twists on its blood supply, killing the tissue. It often looks identical to GI stasis at first — same not eating, same hunched posture — which is why it can be missed. We diagnose it with bloodwork (the liver enzymes shoot up) and ultrasound. There's no clear cause we can point to; it can happen to any rabbit, sometimes after a fall or rough handling, sometimes for no reason at all. The treatment is surgery to remove the dead lobe — and the prognosis is actually quite good if we catch it early.
preventableNote: No clear preventable cause. Some cases follow trauma — avoid rough handling, prevent falls.
prognosis: Excellent with surgical lobectomy (90%+ survival). Medical management alone has 50-70% survival. Untreated → liver failure, death.
prevention: Annual bloodwork in middle-aged rabbits to catch silent rises in ALT/AST. Suspect LLT in any rabbit with sudden anorexia + elevated liver enzymes — don't assume "just stasis."

name: Flystrike (Myiasis)
cause: Blowflies (Lucilia, Calliphora) lay eggs in soiled fur — especially around the perineum where urine or soft caecotrophs have accumulated. Eggs hatch into maggots within hours that burrow into skin and release toxins. Most cases are in obese, arthritic, or dental-disease rabbits that can't reach their hindquarters to groom, or rabbits with diarrhoea. Strongly seasonal — peaks in warm humid months.
script: Flystrike happens when flies lay eggs in damp or soiled fur, and the maggots hatch in just a few hours. By the time you see it, hundreds of maggots may already be burrowing into the skin and releasing toxins. The underlying problem is almost always that the rabbit couldn't groom himself properly — either because he's overweight, his back hurts, or his teeth are bad. We need to clip all the fur, manually remove every maggot, give pain meds and IV fluids. Long-term we need to fix what's stopping him from grooming.
preventableNote: Daily inspection of perineum + immediate cleaning of any soiling. Fly screens. Address obesity / dental / arthritis.
prognosis: Localized: excellent. Extensive (>30% body surface) or septic: guarded to grave.
prevention: Daily butt check in warm months. Fly screens on windows. Maintain ideal weight. Address dental disease + arthritis that prevent self-grooming. Topical cyromazine spray prophylactically in high-risk seasons.

name: Splay Leg
cause: One or more limbs (usually hindlimbs) splay outward and the rabbit cannot stand normally. Causes are mixed: (1) genetic predisposition in some lines — most common in baby rabbits. (2) Slippery flooring during the critical developmental window (3-10 days old) so the hip joints don't form properly. (3) Hip dysplasia. (4) Vitamin D deficiency. (5) Less commonly, neurological disease or muscle injury.
script: Splay leg in baby rabbits is usually a mix of genetics and the surface they were raised on — slippery floors during the first 2 weeks of life prevent the hips from forming properly. There's no cure for the structural problem, but with physical therapy, soft bedding, and weight management many splay-leg rabbits live full happy lives. Affected rabbits and their parents should not be bred from.
preventableNote: Genetic component cannot be prevented. Environmental component (slippery floor) is preventable in young litters.
prognosis: Mild unilateral: live normal life with adaptations. Severe bilateral: poor quality of life — euthanasia may be indicated.
prevention: Non-slip bedding (towel, fleece) for newborn litters. Don't breed affected animals or their parents. Adequate vitamin D in doe's diet during gestation.

name: Coccidiosis (Rabbit)
cause: Protozoan parasites of the genus *Eimeria* — multiple species cause either intestinal (diarrhoea, weight loss) or hepatic (liver damage, jaundice) coccidiosis. Spread via faecal-oral route. Most pet rabbits are exposed as kits and develop immunity, but young, immunocompromised, or stressed rabbits develop clinical disease. Hepatic form is most severe — *E. stiedae* destroys the bile ducts.
script: Coccidiosis is caused by tiny parasites that live in the gut or liver. Almost all rabbits pick them up as babies and develop immunity, but stress or another illness can let them flare up. The liver form is the more serious version — that's what your rabbit has, based on the jaundice and liver enzyme rise. Treatment is toltrazuril or ponazuril, both of which work well. After this episode, we need to disinfect the cage thoroughly because the eggs of the parasite are very hardy.
preventableNote: Reduce reinfection by daily faeces removal + low-stress environment. Probiotic supplementation during recovery.
prognosis: Intestinal: good with treatment. Hepatic: guarded if liver damage is extensive.
prevention: Daily cleaning. Faecal screening of new rabbits. Stress reduction. Quarantine new arrivals 30 days. Routine ponazuril course in litters from positive does.

name: Urolithiasis / Bladder Sludge — Rabbit
cause: Rabbits and guinea pigs absorb dietary calcium efficiently regardless of need and excrete the excess in urine, which is normally chalky white. With dehydration, inadequate exercise, obesity, or a calcium-heavy diet (alfalfa hay/pellets, kale, parsley in excess), the calcium precipitates into a thick sludge or solid stones that block the bladder or urethra. More common in middle-aged neutered males.
script: Rabbits and guinea pigs handle calcium completely differently from us — they absorb all of it from their food and dump the extra into urine. Normally it comes out as harmless chalky urine. But when she's dehydrated, doesn't move around much, or eats too much high-calcium food like alfalfa, the calcium becomes a thick sludge in the bladder or even hard stones. We may need to flush the bladder out under anaesthesia. Long-term, we'll switch to a low-calcium diet, add more water (wet greens, water fountain), and encourage exercise.
preventableNote: Diet + hydration + exercise are all controllable.
prognosis: Sludge: good with flushing + dietary change. Obstructing stones: surgical removal required, good prognosis.
prevention: Switch from alfalfa to grass hay (Timothy/Orchard) after 6 months. Limit high-Ca greens (kale, parsley, dandelion). Water bowl AND bottle (some prefer bowls = drink more). Daily exercise. Maintain lean BCS.

name: Pseudopregnancy (Rabbit)
cause: An intact doe ovulates in response to mounting (by a male or another female). If fertilization doesn't occur (or if the male was sterile), the corpus luteum still produces progesterone for ~16-18 days, mimicking pregnancy. The doe builds a nest, plucks her own fur, becomes territorial, and may produce milk. Recurrent pseudopregnancy increases risk of uterine adenocarcinoma + pyometra.
script: Pseudopregnancy is when a doe's body acts like she's pregnant even though she isn't — the nest-building, fur-pulling, and milk are all because her hormones genuinely think she is. It resolves on its own in 2-3 weeks. The bigger issue is that recurrent pseudopregnancies raise her risk of uterine cancer and uterine infection — which is the strongest reason to spay her once this episode resolves.
preventableNote: Spaying eliminates risk completely. Single-housing or single-sex pairs reduce triggers.
prognosis: Resolves spontaneously in 16-18 days. Recurrent episodes increase uterine pathology risk.
prevention: Spay at 4-6 months. Avoid housing intact females with mounting males (even neutered males may still mount). Address aggression that triggers same-sex mounting.

name: Pregnancy Toxemia (Guinea Pig)
cause: Late-pregnancy or post-partum guinea pigs develop hypoglycemia, hypocalcemia, and ketosis. Two forms: (1) **Metabolic** — late-pregnancy sow with multiple large fetuses + obesity + sudden fasting (anorexia from stress or illness). (2) **Circulatory** — uterine compression of major vessels in late pregnancy. Sows bred for the first time at > 8 months old are at extreme risk because the pubic symphysis has already fused.
script: Pregnancy toxemia is a deadly metabolic crash that happens in late pregnancy or just after birth. The baby pigs use so much energy that mom's blood sugar drops, and if she stops eating for any reason — stress, illness, even just not liking the new pellet brand — her body starts burning fat in a toxic way. She can die within 24 hours. This is also why we tell people never to breed a guinea pig for the first time after 8 months old — her pelvis fuses and she can't deliver.
preventableNote: Maintain ideal weight pre-breeding. Don't breed sows > 8 months old for first litter. Continuous food supply. Minimize stress in late pregnancy.
prognosis: Guarded to grave even with aggressive treatment. ~50% mortality.
prevention: Breed first litter < 8 months old. Body condition score 5/9 (not obese). Constant access to favorite foods + fresh water + Vit C in late pregnancy. Minimize handling/stress in last 2 weeks.

name: Cervical Lymphadenitis (Guinea Pig)
cause: *Streptococcus zooepidemicus* is the most common cause of "lumps" in guinea pigs — bacterial abscesses in the cervical lymph nodes. Bacteria enter through small mouth lacerations (from spiky hay or splinters from chewing) and migrate to the regional lymph nodes. Spreads cage-to-cage by direct contact or via shared bedding. Can also cause pneumonia, septicemia, or meningitis if not treated.
script: These lumps under the jaw are abscesses in the lymph nodes — caused by a bacterium called Strep zoo that gets in through tiny cuts in the mouth, often from spiky pieces of hay. They're usually not contagious to humans, but they can spread between guinea pigs. We need to surgically remove the entire abscess capsule (lancing alone doesn't cure it because guinea pig pus is thick and cheesy), then give antibiotics. Keep him separated from other pigs until fully healed.
preventableNote: Soft hay (no spiky pieces), inspect mouth weekly, quarantine new pigs 30 days.
prognosis: Good with complete surgical excision + antibiotics. Recurrence if abscess not fully removed.
prevention: Quality soft hay (no coarse alfalfa stems). Quarantine new pigs. Don't share bedding/bowls between cages. Treat oral cuts early.

name: Trixacarus Mange (Guinea Pig)
cause: *Trixacarus caviae* is a sarcoptic mite specific to guinea pigs. Most pigs carry it asymptomatically and only develop clinical disease under stress, immunosuppression, or with another illness. The mites burrow into skin causing intense itching, hair loss, thickened scaly skin, and sometimes seizures from the pain. **Zoonotic** — can cause temporary itchy rash in humans handling affected pigs.
script: These mites live on the skin of almost all guinea pigs without causing problems, until stress or another illness lets them multiply out of control. The itching is so severe that some pigs actually have seizures from the pain — that's how bad it can get. Treatment is selamectin or ivermectin every 2 weeks for three doses. Also worth mentioning — these mites can cause a temporary itchy rash on your skin too if you handle her without washing afterward, so gloves are a good idea until she's clear.
preventableNote: Almost all guinea pigs carry the mite — cannot prevent exposure. Reduce stress to prevent flare-ups.
prognosis: Excellent with selamectin or ivermectin × 3 doses 2 weeks apart.
prevention: Quarantine new pigs 30 days + treat prophylactically. Stress reduction (good husbandry, social pairs). Routine bath/inspection.

name: Epizootic Catarrhal Enteritis (ECE)
cause: A coronavirus (FECV) that causes severe inflammatory enteritis. Highly contagious — spreads through ferret colonies and shows in newly-introduced ferrets within 2-3 weeks. Older ferrets are most affected — younger ones often have mild signs but become long-term shedders. Characteristic bright green mucousy diarrhoea ("green slime") gives the disease its nickname.
script: ECE is a viral enteritis — the "green slime" appearance of the diarrhoea is very distinctive. It's caused by a coronavirus that's extremely contagious between ferrets. Older ferrets get the sickest. There's no specific antiviral but supportive care — fluids, anti-nausea, force feeding — usually pulls them through over 1-2 weeks. The key going forward is strict quarantine of any new ferret for 30 days, because ones that recover can shed the virus for months.
preventableNote: Strict 30-day quarantine of new ferrets eliminates risk. No vaccine.
prognosis: Adult ferrets: 70-90% survive with supportive care. Geriatric or concurrent disease: guarded.
prevention: 30-60 day quarantine for any new ferret. No mixing with strange ferrets at boarding/shows.

name: Estrogen Toxicity (Intact Jill)
cause: Female ferrets are induced ovulators — they only ovulate if bred. An intact jill that comes into heat and is not bred stays in estrus continuously, with sustained high estrogen levels that suppress the bone marrow. Within 4-6 weeks of unbred estrus, the marrow stops producing red blood cells, white cells, and platelets — fatal aplastic anemia. Vulvar swelling that persists > 2 weeks is a red flag.
script: Female ferrets that aren't spayed and don't get bred will stay in heat for weeks or months, and the constant high estrogen poisons the bone marrow. Within 4-6 weeks they stop making blood cells and become severely anemic — often fatal. The vulvar swelling you noticed is the warning sign. Treatment depends on how anemic she already is — mild cases respond to a deslorelin implant or spaying; severe cases need blood transfusions. Going forward, every female ferret should either be spayed or implanted before her first heat.
preventableNote: Spay or deslorelin implant before/during first heat = 100% prevention.
