prognosis: Mild (PCV > 25%): good with hCG / spaying. Severe (PCV < 15%): grave even with transfusion.
prevention: Spay before first heat (~5-6 months) OR deslorelin implant for behavioral / non-surgical option.

name: Hedgehog Mites (Caparinia)
cause: *Caparinia tripilis* is the most common skin parasite of African pygmy hedgehogs — many pet hedgehogs are infested from the breeder. Causes excessive quill loss (different from normal seasonal "quilling"), dry flaky skin, head shaking, and self-trauma. Often found incidentally during a routine exam.
script: Mites are extremely common in pet hedgehogs — many are infested from the breeder before you ever got him. The excessive quill loss and flaky skin you noticed are typical. We treat with ivermectin or selamectin every 2 weeks for three doses, plus a thorough cage clean. After treatment the new quills will grow in normally and the skin will heal up.
preventableNote: Buy from reputable breeders. Quarantine new hedgehogs 30 days.
prognosis: Excellent with treatment. Quills regrow over weeks.
prevention: Quarantine new hedgehogs. Source from breeders who treat preventively. Bedding change weekly. Clean exercise wheel.

name: Hedgehog Cardiomyopathy (DCM)
cause: Dilated cardiomyopathy affects 30-40% of pet hedgehogs by age 3-5 years. Cause is unclear — suspected combination of genetics, taurine deficiency (cat food without taurine), and obesity. The heart muscle stretches thin and pumps weakly, leading to congestive heart failure. Often diagnosed late because hedgehogs hide their illness well.
script: Heart disease is unfortunately very common in older hedgehogs — about 1 in 3 are affected. We don't fully understand why, but genetics, diet (cat food without taurine), and obesity all seem to contribute. Hedgehogs hide their illness well, which is why we often diagnose it late. We can't cure it but with diuretics, ACE inhibitors, and pimobendan we can often add 6-12 good months. We'll also adjust her diet and reduce weight if she's overweight.
preventableNote: Diet quality + weight management may help. Cannot prevent genetic predisposition.
prognosis: Median survival 6-12 months from diagnosis with treatment.
prevention: High-quality cat food labeled with taurine (or hedgehog-specific kibble). Maintain ideal weight. Annual cardiac auscultation from age 2. Echocardiogram if any murmur or signs.

name: Sour Crop / Crop Stasis
cause: The crop empties slowly or not at all, food ferments, and yeast (typically *Candida albicans*) overgrows the warm fermenting environment. Common in: (1) Hand-fed baby parrots (formula too cold, too thick, or fed when the crop is still full from last feed). (2) Adult birds with reduced motility from stress, illness, or medication (long antibiotic course). (3) Birds with proventricular disease (PDD).
script: Sour crop is when food sits in the crop too long and ferments — yeast then overgrows in the sweet warm environment. In a hand-fed baby it's usually a formula issue: too cold, too thick, or fed before the previous meal has emptied. In an adult bird it usually means something else is slowing the gut down — stress, infection, or the antibiotic she's been on disrupting the normal flora. Treatment is to empty the crop, give antifungal medication (nystatin or fluconazole), and address the underlying cause.
preventableNote: Hand-feed correctly. Probiotic during antibiotic courses. Verify crop empties between feeds.
prognosis: Excellent with prompt treatment. Aspiration pneumonia is the main risk.
prevention: Hand-feed formula at 38-40°C, correct consistency. Confirm crop empties between feeds. Probiotics during any abx course. Reduce stress.

name: Feather Destructive Behavior (FDB)
cause: Multi-factorial — almost always a combination of medical and behavioral. Medical: PBFD, giardia, heavy metal toxicity, hypothyroidism, allergic skin disease, hypocalcemia (African Grey), low humidity, gonadal hyperactivity, infection. Behavioral: boredom, lack of foraging/enrichment, sleep deprivation, social isolation, anxiety, sexual frustration, learned attention-seeking. Cockatoos, African Greys, and quaker parrots are most predisposed.
script: Feather plucking is one of the hardest problems we deal with because there's rarely a single cause. Medical causes — infections, parasites, allergies, low calcium, hormones — need to be ruled out first with bloodwork and tests. But even when medical causes are treated, behavioral factors usually still drive it: not enough foraging, lack of sleep (parrots need 10-12 hours of true darkness), social isolation, or hormonal frustration. This usually takes months of patient work to address, not weeks.
preventableNote: Excellent environmental enrichment from young age + 10-12h sleep + species-appropriate social interaction + foraging-based feeding.
prognosis: Variable. Some birds recover fully; many become chronic pluckers despite all interventions.
prevention: Foraging toys + complex feeding (not bowl-fed). 10-12h covered sleep. Pellet diet not seed. UV light. Limit petting to head only (back/wing petting = sexual stimulation). Annual health check including bloodwork.

name: Hepatic Lipidosis (Bird)
cause: Excess dietary fat (sunflower seeds, peanuts, oil seeds) + sedentary cage life + obesity = fat infiltration of the liver over years. Budgies and cockatiels on seed-only diets are the classic patients. Liver fails when fat replaces functional tissue → bleeding tendency, neurological signs, sudden death. Often diagnosed at necropsy.
script: Fatty liver disease is the bird version of what we see in overweight humans on poor diets. Years of seed-heavy eating, especially the fatty seeds like sunflower and peanut, combined with lack of flight exercise, lets fat infiltrate the liver. Eventually the liver can't do its job and the bird becomes unable to clot properly, becomes neurological, or simply dies suddenly. The damage we've already done can be partially reversed with diet change, but only if we start now.
preventableNote: Pellet-based diet + flight time from young age = highly preventable.
prognosis: Reversible if caught early + diet changed. Late cases (overt liver failure, ascites) have poor prognosis.
prevention: Pellet diet ≥ 70% from young. Convert seed-eaters gradually (3-6 months). NO sunflower or peanuts as staple. Out-of-cage flight time daily. Annual bloodwork (bile acids, AST) in seed-converted budgies/cockatiels.

name: Avian Gout
cause: Uric acid (the bird/reptile equivalent of urea) crystallizes in joints (articular gout — painful swollen joints, won't perch) or organs (visceral gout — found at necropsy in liver, kidneys, heart). Causes: chronic dehydration, high-protein diet (excess animal protein in granivores), nephrotoxic drugs (aminoglycosides, NSAIDs, sulfonamides), or pre-existing kidney disease. Once visible, kidney damage is already advanced.
script: Gout in birds is what happens when the kidneys can't clear uric acid fast enough — it crystallizes in joints or organs. The painful swollen joints she's standing on are full of urate crystals. This means her kidneys have been struggling for a long time, often because of chronic dehydration, too much animal protein in the diet, or sometimes from medications. Treatment is allopurinol, allopurinol, supportive care, and addressing the kidney issue if we can. The prognosis is guarded.
preventableNote: Adequate hydration + appropriate protein level + avoid nephrotoxic drugs.
prognosis: Articular: manageable with allopurinol. Visceral: usually grave by the time it's detected.
prevention: Constant fresh water. Pellet diet (avoid high-protein dog/cat food in granivores). Hydration support during illness. Avoid aminoglycosides + NSAIDs in dehydrated birds.

name: Goiter (Budgerigar)
cause: Iodine deficiency in seed-fed budgies causes the thyroid to enlarge as it tries to make hormone with inadequate raw material. The enlarged thyroid presses on the trachea (causing wheezing, "squeaking" voice change) and crop (regurgitation). Almost exclusively seen in budgies on millet/seed-only diets without iodine supplementation. Easily reversed if caught early.
script: Goiter in budgies is caused by not enough iodine in the diet — seeds have almost none. The thyroid swells trying to make hormone without enough iodine, and the swelling presses on the windpipe — which is why her voice has changed and she's wheezing. Treatment is iodine supplementation in the water for 2-4 weeks plus a long-term switch to a pellet diet that has iodine built in.
preventableNote: 100% preventable with pellet diet or iodine-containing mineral block.
prognosis: Excellent if caught before tracheal collapse. Late cases may need debulking surgery.
prevention: Pellet diet ≥ 70%. Iodine block in cage. Drinking water iodine supplement weekly for seed-eaters. ⚠ Avoid goitrogenic vegetables that block iodine uptake: cabbage, kale, collard greens, Brussels sprouts, broccoli, cauliflower, turnip and soybean. Iodine-deficiency goiter also occurs in carnivores fed only beef/freshwater fish (very low iodine — a lion needs ~1,000 µg/day vs only ~33 µg in beef); marine fish, liver and seaweed are iodine-rich.

name: Bumblefoot (Avian Pododermatitis)
cause: Pressure necrosis of the plantar foot surface from prolonged pressure on hard / uniform / inappropriate perches. Sandpaper perches (cause abrasion), single-diameter perches (no variation in pressure points), wire flooring, and obesity all contribute. Common in heavy birds (raptors, parrots, chickens). The abrasion becomes infected (typically *Staphylococcus aureus*), forms an abscess, and progresses to osteomyelitis.
script: Bumblefoot is caused by pressure on the bottom of the feet, often from perches that are all the same size or material. The skin breaks down, bacteria get in, and we end up with what you see today — a swollen infected pad. The cornerstone of treatment is changing the environment: varied perch sizes, no sandpaper covers, lower weight, soft footing. Antibiotics alone won't cure it if the underlying pressure stays the same.
preventableNote: Varied perch sizes (different diameter natural branches), no sandpaper, weight management.
prognosis: Mild (Grade 1-2): excellent with husbandry change. Severe (Grade 4-5 with osteomyelitis): guarded.
prevention: Multiple perches of different natural-branch diameters. NO sandpaper perches. Padded perches for known risk birds (Pionus, Amazons). Maintain ideal body weight. Inspect feet monthly.

name: Trichomoniasis (Canker)
cause: A protozoan parasite (*Trichomonas gallinae*) of the upper digestive tract — mouth, throat, crop, sometimes liver. Pigeons are the natural host (often subclinical). Raptors get it from eating infected pigeons ("frounce"). Pet birds catch it from shared water bowls with pigeons or other infected birds. Creates characteristic yellow caseous plaques in the mouth and throat that can obstruct breathing.
script: Canker is caused by a tiny parasite that lives in the throat and crop. Pigeons carry it normally — your bird probably picked it up from shared water with wild birds, or in the case of a raptor, from eating an infected pigeon. The yellow plaques you can see in his mouth are the classic finding. Treatment is metronidazole or ronidazole for 7-10 days, and it usually clears completely.
preventableNote: No outdoor access to wild bird water sources. Don't feed raptors live pigeons.
prognosis: Excellent with metronidazole/ronidazole. Severe cases with airway obstruction: guarded.
prevention: Cover outdoor water from wild birds. Quarantine new pigeons + screen with wet mount. Don't feed raptors live/recently-killed pigeons (freeze meat ≥ 1 week to kill protozoa).

name: Cnemidocoptes (Scaly Face/Leg Mite)
cause: *Knemidokoptes pilae* is a burrowing mite specific to budgies, canaries, and some other passerines. Causes thickened, honeycombed crusts on the beak, cere, eyelids, legs, and feet. Usually transmitted parent-to-chick. Many birds carry it asymptomatically; clinical disease appears when immunity drops with stress, age, or other illness.
script: Scaly face is a mite that burrows under the skin of the beak and feet, creating the honeycomb appearance you see. Most budgies pick it up from their parents as chicks. It only becomes obvious when the immune system is stressed. Treatment is ivermectin — one or two doses usually clears it completely. The crusts will gradually slough off over a few weeks.
preventableNote: Treat breeding stock. Quarantine new birds.
prognosis: Excellent with ivermectin × 1-2 doses.
prevention: Annual exam. Treat any visible scale early. Don't mix infected with healthy.

name: Reptile Gout
cause: Same mechanism as bird gout — urate crystallization from dehydration + inappropriate protein. Especially common in herbivorous reptiles (iguana, tortoise) fed dog/cat food or high-animal-protein diet; and in any reptile chronically underhydrated. Also common with nephrotoxic drugs (aminoglycosides) or chronic kidney disease.
script: Gout in reptiles is almost always a husbandry problem — either too much protein in the diet, especially animal protein in plant-eaters, or chronic dehydration. The crystals in the joints are extremely painful. Treatment is allopurinol plus a major husbandry overhaul. Once joint damage has happened it doesn't reverse, but we can stop progression.
preventableNote: Appropriate diet for species + hydration.
prognosis: Articular: manageable. Visceral: usually fatal.
prevention: Strict herbivore diet for iguanas/tortoises (NO animal protein). Constant water access. Humidity for tropical species. Soak chelonians weekly.

name: Snake Mites (Ophionyssus)
cause: *Ophionyssus natricis* is the most common ectoparasite of captive snakes. Tiny black/red mites visible on scales (especially around eyes, head, and under chin) and floating in the water dish. Hides in cage cracks during the day. Transmitted via shared housing or contaminated supplies. Heavy infestation causes anaemia, restlessness, excessive soaking, and can transmit blood parasites (Aeromonas septicemia, IBD).
script: Mites are very common in pet snakes, especially recently-acquired ones. The little black or red dots you see on her scales — particularly around her eyes — are adult mites. They'll be hiding all over the cage during the day. Treatment requires three parts: treat the snake (ivermectin spray or oral selamectin), treat the cage (everything porous discarded, hard surfaces disinfected), and treat the room for 6+ weeks to break the life cycle. Mites can also transmit serious blood diseases, so this is more than just an itching problem.
preventableNote: Strict quarantine 90 days for any new snake. Inspect at every cage clean.
prognosis: Excellent with treatment + environmental decontamination. Untreated → anaemia + secondary infection.
prevention: 90-day quarantine of new snakes. Inspect water dish + chin scales weekly. Bleach cage between snakes. Avoid wood/porous decor in mite-prone collections.

name: Inclusion Body Disease (IBD)
cause: A reptarenavirus that infects boas and pythons. Boa constrictors can carry it for years without signs; pythons get sick quickly and die. Transmission: snake mites (suspected vector), direct contact, possibly mother-to-offspring. Causes characteristic intracytoplasmic inclusion bodies in many tissues. Once a collection is positive, the virus is essentially permanent.
script: IBD is a viral disease that's become a nightmare for boa and python keepers. Boas can carry it silently for years; pythons die quickly. There's no cure and we don't have a reliable live test — diagnosis often comes from biopsy or at necropsy. If she's positive, every other snake in the collection should be considered exposed. The best we can do is supportive care and prevent spread by aggressive mite control and isolation of any new snakes.
preventableNote: Strict mite control + 6+ month quarantine for new boids. PCR test new arrivals.
prognosis: Boa: slow decline over 1-3 years. Python: rapid (weeks-months). No cure.
prevention: 90+ day quarantine. PCR or biopsy testing of new boids. Mite control. Don't mix boas + pythons.

name: Atadenovirus (Bearded Dragon)
cause: A DNA virus that infects bearded dragons, especially young ones. Transmitted by faecal-oral route, often dam-to-offspring. Affects multiple organ systems — liver, immune system, brain. Many adult dragons are subclinical carriers. Babies present with "failure to thrive" — small, weak, dying despite good husbandry. Adults occasionally present with neurological signs ("star-gazing").
script: Atadenovirus is unfortunately very common in bearded dragons, especially babies. Many adult dragons carry it without showing signs but pass it to their offspring. There's no cure — we can only support nutrition and treat any secondary infections. If you have other dragons at home, they're probably all exposed already. Going forward, we recommend not adding more dragons to the household and not breeding affected ones.
preventableNote: PCR screening of breeding stock. Quarantine + screen new dragons. Most pet store stock is positive.
prognosis: Babies: poor (high mortality). Adults: variable, often live years as carriers.
prevention: PCR-screen breeding adults. Don't breed positive dragons. Quarantine all new dragons 90 days with PCR test.

name: Yellow Fungus Disease (CANV)
cause: *Nannizziopsis* (formerly CANV — Chrysosporium anamorph) is a fungus that aggressively invades reptile skin. Highly contagious between reptiles. Causes characteristic yellow-brown crusts that progress to deep ulcers exposing bone. Often introduced via new reptiles or contaminated equipment. Treatment is difficult — voriconazole, terbinafine, and aggressive wound care for months.
script: Yellow fungus is one of the most feared diseases in bearded dragons — it spreads easily between reptiles and the prognosis is guarded even with aggressive treatment. We'll start oral voriconazole for at least 60 days plus topical wound care. Strict isolation from any other reptiles is critical, and the equipment used on her should never touch another dragon. Even with treatment, mortality is significant.
preventableNote: Strict 90-day quarantine + fungal culture screening.
prognosis: Guarded — 30-50% mortality even with treatment. Early lesions have better prognosis.
prevention: 90-day quarantine + skin scrape/culture screening of new dragons. Dedicated equipment per reptile. Disinfect with F10 or bleach.

name: Gastric Dilatation-Volvulus (GDV)
cause: The stomach fills with gas/fluid and then rotates (torsion) on its axis, cutting off blood supply and the ability to vomit or belch. Without surgery, fatal within hours. Risk factors: deep-chested large breeds (Great Dane, German Shepherd, Standard Poodle, Weimaraner, Boxer), single large meal, eating rapidly, exercise immediately after eating, raised food bowls, stress, anxiety, age > 7.
script: GDV — what people call "bloat" — is when the stomach fills with gas and then twists, cutting off blood supply. It's an absolute emergency. Without surgery in the next few hours she will die. Deep-chested breeds like yours are at especially high risk. Once we get her through this surgery, we should consider a "gastropexy" — tacking the stomach to the body wall — to prevent it from twisting again. For predisposed breeds we often do prophylactic gastropexy at the time of spay or neuter.
preventableNote: Risk reduction: multiple small meals, slow-feeder bowls, no exercise 1h before/after eating, prophylactic gastropexy at spay/neuter for at-risk breeds.
prognosis: With prompt surgery (< 5h from onset): 80% survival. Each hour delay drops survival. Without surgery: ~100% mortality.
prevention: Prophylactic gastropexy at spay/neuter for Great Danes, GSDs, Weimaraners, etc. 2-3 smaller meals/day. Slow-feeder bowl. No vigorous exercise 1h pre/post meal. Ground-level food bowl (not elevated).

name: Canine Atopic Dermatitis
cause: A genetic disorder of the skin barrier + immune system. Allergens (pollen, dust mites, mold, sometimes food proteins) penetrate the abnormal skin barrier and trigger inflammation. Worst-affected breeds: West Highland White, French Bulldog, Bulldog, Boxer, Shar Pei, Labrador Retriever, Golden Retriever. Usually starts age 1-3 years. Lifelong condition — manageable but not curable.
script: Atopic dermatitis is the dog equivalent of eczema in humans — it's a genetic condition where the skin barrier doesn't work properly, allowing allergens to penetrate and trigger inflammation. There's no cure, but with modern medications (apoquel, cytopoint, immunotherapy) we can usually get most dogs comfortable without long-term steroids. Bath frequency, omega-3 supplements, and identifying any food triggers also help. This will be a lifelong management situation, not a quick fix.
preventableNote: Cannot prevent genetic predisposition. Avoid breeding affected dogs. Early antigen exposure may shape immune development.
prognosis: Excellent quality of life with modern therapy. Lifelong management.
prevention: Annual allergy workup. Weekly bath with prescription shampoo. Omega-3 daily. Apoquel/Cytopoint or immunotherapy as needed. Hypoallergenic diet trial.

name: Anal Sac Disease
cause: Anal sacs (two small glands beside the anus) normally empty during defecation. When stool is too soft to express them, when the dog is overweight, or when the sac duct is blocked, the secretion thickens, gets impacted, becomes infected, and may abscess. Small breeds with anal sac disease often need lifelong manual expression every 4-6 weeks.
script: The two glands beside the anus normally empty when she poops. When her stool isn't firm enough — too much soft food, too little fiber, or being overweight — the glands don't express on their own and the secretion gets thick and impacted. That's what's causing the scooting and licking. We'll express them today, but if this becomes a regular problem we may need to add fiber to her diet, address weight, or do scheduled expressions every few weeks. Surgery to remove the sacs is a last resort.
preventableNote: High-fiber diet + ideal weight + firm stool reduces frequency.
prognosis: Manageable. Recurrent abscess → surgical anal sacculectomy curative but small risk of fecal incontinence.
prevention: Adequate dietary fiber (pumpkin, psyllium, prescription fiber diet). Maintain ideal weight. Routine expression q4-8 weeks in predisposed dogs.

name: Periodontal Disease
cause: Plaque bacteria accumulate on teeth → mineralize into tartar → trigger gingivitis → progress to periodontitis with bone loss around teeth. By age 3, 80% of dogs and 70% of cats have some degree of periodontal disease. Small breeds and brachycephalic breeds are worst affected because crowded teeth retain more plaque. Cats also have a unique condition called feline tooth resorption that destroys teeth from the inside.
script: Periodontal disease is incredibly common — by age 3, 4 out of 5 dogs have some degree of it. The bacteria from infected gums constantly enter the bloodstream and can damage the kidneys, liver, and heart valves over time. Daily tooth brushing is the gold standard for prevention but most owners struggle with that. Annual or biannual professional dental cleaning under anaesthesia is the next best thing. The "anaesthesia-free dental cleanings" sometimes offered are not effective for the part of the tooth under the gumline that matters most.
preventableNote: Daily tooth brushing + annual prophy = highly preventable.
prognosis: Treatable but progressive without ongoing care. Extracted teeth don't grow back — preventive care is much better than reactive.
prevention: Daily brushing with enzymatic dog/cat toothpaste (never human toothpaste — xylitol). VOHC-accepted dental chews. Annual prophy under anaesthesia. Avoid hard chews (antlers, bones) that fracture teeth.

name: Cranial Cruciate Ligament Rupture (CCL)
cause: Degenerative — the ligament gradually weakens over months to years before snapping. Risk factors: obesity (single biggest modifiable factor), breed predisposition (Labrador, Rottweiler, Boxer, Bullmastiff), early neuter (predisposes large breeds), poor conformation, jumping/twisting injuries on top of an already-weakened ligament. The other knee often ruptures within 1-2 years if not addressed.
script: In dogs, cruciate rupture is usually a slow degenerative process — the ligament weakens over months before finally tearing. Even though it seems like she "just hurt it jumping off the couch," the damage was building for a long time. Surgery (TPLO or TTA) is the gold standard for medium/large dogs and offers the best long-term outcome. Without surgery, arthritis will set in within months. Also — the other knee has a 50% chance of doing the same thing within 1-2 years, so we should monitor it closely.
preventableNote: Maintain lean body weight. Discuss timing of neuter in large breeds. Avoid extreme exercise without conditioning.
prognosis: Surgery: 90% return to good function. No surgery: chronic arthritis, lifelong lameness.
prevention: Lean body weight (single biggest factor). Discuss delayed neuter (12-18 months) in large breeds. Regular controlled exercise. Avoid weekend-warrior intense activity.

name: Tick-Borne Disease
cause: Bacterial (Ehrlichia, Anaplasma) or protozoal (Babesia, Hepatozoon) infections transmitted by tick bites. Geographic distribution varies. Tick must be attached for 24-48 hours to transmit most pathogens — daily tick checks can prevent infection. Causes fever, lethargy, lymphadenopathy, thrombocytopenia (bleeding), anemia, and chronic immune-mediated disease.
script: Tick-borne diseases come from infected ticks — the tick has to be attached for at least 24 hours to transmit, which is why we recommend daily tick checks during tick season. The good news is that with prompt treatment most dogs respond well to doxycycline. Going forward, year-round tick prevention is much easier than treating disease — there are excellent monthly products that kill ticks before they can transmit.
preventableNote: Monthly tick prevention (NexGard, Bravecto, Simparica) eliminates most risk.
prognosis: Acute: good with doxycycline. Chronic ehrlichiosis with pancytopenia: guarded.
prevention: Year-round monthly tick prevention. Daily tick check after outdoor exposure. Annual SNAP 4Dx (heartworm + 3 tick diseases) screening.

name: Canine Pancreatitis
cause: Inflammation of the pancreas. Most common trigger: a single high-fat meal (Christmas turkey, leftover bacon, garbage raid). Other triggers: obesity (chronic risk), hypertriglyceridemia (Miniature Schnauzer especially), medications (potassium bromide, asparaginase), trauma, infection, toxins. The pancreas digests itself + surrounding tissues, causing severe pain, vomiting, and sometimes systemic inflammatory response.
script: Pancreatitis is inflammation of the pancreas — most commonly triggered by a fatty meal. Even one episode of fatty human food (bacon, leftover meat trimmings, butter) can set it off. The pancreas essentially digests itself, which is why she's in so much pain. Treatment is IV fluids, strong pain control, anti-nausea meds, and gradual reintroduction of low-fat food. Going forward — no human food, ever, and a low-fat diet for life because once she's had one episode, she's much more likely to have another.
preventableNote: No human food (especially fatty). Low-fat diet in predisposed breeds.
prognosis: Mild: full recovery in 5-7 days. Severe with systemic inflammation: guarded.
prevention: No fatty human food — ever. Low-fat prescription diet in predisposed breeds (Schnauzer). Maintain lean weight. Monitor triglycerides in Schnauzers.

name: Brachycephalic Obstructive Airway Syndrome (BOAS)
cause: Genetic conformational disorder of flat-faced breeds (French Bulldog, English Bulldog, Pug, Boston Terrier, Persian cat, Exotic Shorthair cat). Multiple anatomical abnormalities: narrow nostrils (stenotic nares), elongated soft palate, everted laryngeal saccules, hypoplastic trachea. The dog has to work hard to breathe at rest, which gets worse with heat, excitement, exercise, or sleep apnea.
script: BOAS is the result of breeding for the flat face — the soft tissues inside the head haven't shrunk to match, so they obstruct the airway. The snoring, snorting, and difficulty in the heat are all from this obstruction. Surgery to widen the nostrils and trim the soft palate is the best treatment — earlier is better, ideally before age 2. Without surgery, the obstruction progressively worsens and significantly shortens life expectancy.
preventableNote: Genetic / breed conformation. The only true prevention is not breeding extreme brachycephalic conformations.
prognosis: Surgical correction (BOAS surgery): dramatic improvement, near-normal life. Untreated: progressive worsening, heat-related death common.
prevention: BOAS-grading before breeding. Surgical correction (rhinoplasty + staphylectomy) early (< 2 years). Lean weight. Air conditioning in summer. Harness not collar.

name: Tracheal Collapse
cause: Weakness of the cartilage rings of the trachea — they flatten during breathing, causing the characteristic "goose honk" cough. Almost exclusively a small-breed disease — Yorkshire Terrier, Pomeranian, Chihuahua, Maltese. Genetic, with onset usually middle-aged. Aggravated by obesity, neck collars, second-hand smoke, allergies, heat, and excitement. Generally slowly progressive.
script: Tracheal collapse is a genetic problem in small breeds where the cartilage of the windpipe is weak. The "honking" cough happens because the trachea flattens with each breath. We can't fix the cartilage but we can manage the cough with cough suppressants, weight management, switching to a harness (never use a neck collar again), and reducing excitement triggers. For severe cases there's a tracheal stent procedure but it's a specialty referral.
preventableNote: Genetic. Risk reduction: maintain lean weight, harness instead of collar, avoid second-hand smoke.
prognosis: Mild-moderate: good QoL with medical management for years. Severe: guarded — may need stent.
prevention: Harness only (no neck collar) in predisposed breeds from puppyhood. Lean body weight. Smoke-free home. Avoid excitement triggers.

name: Kennel Cough (CIRD)
also: Canine infectious respiratory disease complex, Bordetella
cause: Multiple pathogens together: *Bordetella bronchiseptica* (bacterium), canine parainfluenza virus, canine adenovirus-2, canine respiratory coronavirus, mycoplasma. Spreads through aerosols in dog-dense environments (boarding, daycare, dog parks, grooming, shelters). Causes a dry hacking cough often described as a "goose honk" or as if "something is stuck in the throat."
script: Kennel cough is a complex of several viruses and bacteria that spread between dogs in close quarters — boarding, dog daycare, the dog park. The honking cough is classic. Most cases are mild and self-limit in 1-2 weeks, but in puppies, geriatrics, or brachycephalic breeds it can progress to pneumonia. The Bordetella vaccine reduces severity but doesn't completely prevent it. Going forward, vaccinate before any boarding or social events.
preventableNote: Bordetella vaccine + parainfluenza + adeno-2 (in DHPP) reduces severity but doesn't eliminate risk.
prognosis: Mild cases: self-resolve 1-2 weeks. Pneumonia in vulnerable dogs: guarded.
prevention: Bordetella vaccine (intranasal or oral) annually + booster 1 week before boarding. Avoid high-density dog environments during outbreaks. Maintain DHPP vaccination.

name: Feline Hepatic Lipidosis
cause: An overweight cat that stops eating for any reason (illness, stress, new home, change of food, hospitalization) mobilizes body fat to the liver faster than the liver can process it. The fat infiltrates and clogs the hepatocytes, causing liver failure within days. Unique to cats because of their inability to handle large protein/fat shifts. Any overweight cat that hasn't eaten in 2-3 days is at risk.
script: Hepatic lipidosis is a uniquely feline disease — when an overweight cat stops eating, even just for a few days, the body sends fat to the liver faster than the liver can handle, and the liver fails. The triggering illness is often something minor — a stressful move, a new pet, even just not liking new food — but the consequences are severe. Treatment requires placing a feeding tube and force-feeding for 4-6 weeks while the liver recovers. Most cats survive with aggressive treatment, but it's a long road.
preventableNote: Maintain lean body weight. Any cat skipping meals for > 2 days needs urgent veterinary attention.
prognosis: 80-90% survive with aggressive feeding tube management. Without tube feeding: most die.
prevention: Maintain ideal weight throughout life. Never crash-diet a cat. Track food intake daily for cats > 5 kg. Any 2-day anorexia = veterinary visit, not "wait and see."

name: Hypertrophic Cardiomyopathy (HCM)
cause: The most common heart disease of cats — heart muscle thickens abnormally, reducing chamber volume and impairing relaxation. Strongly genetic in Maine Coon (MyBPC3 mutation), Ragdoll (different MyBPC3 mutation), and predisposed in Persian, British Shorthair, Sphynx. Many affected cats appear completely healthy until they suddenly develop heart failure, throw a clot (saddle thrombus → hindlimb paralysis), or die suddenly.
script: HCM is the most common heart disease in cats and one of the most frightening, because so many affected cats appear completely healthy right up until they collapse or have a stroke. In some breeds — Maine Coon, Ragdoll especially — it's genetic with available DNA tests. Once we diagnose it, we can use medications to slow progression, prevent dangerous clots with anti-clotting medication, and manage heart failure if it develops. But it's a lifelong disease.
preventableNote: Genetic. Breeders should DNA test parents.
prognosis: Asymptomatic: many years of good life. Once heart failure or thromboembolism: median 6-18 months.
prevention: DNA testing of Maine Coons and Ragdolls before breeding. Annual cardiac auscultation. Echocardiogram in predisposed breeds from age 1.

name: Feline Asthma
cause: Allergic inflammation of the lower airways — same disease as human asthma. Triggered by inhaled allergens: cat litter dust (especially clumping clay), cigarette smoke, scented candles, room sprays, perfumes, dust, mold, pollen. Genetic predisposition in Siamese. Without treatment, chronic inflammation causes permanent airway remodeling and lung scarring.
script: Feline asthma is the same disease as asthma in people — the airways become inflamed and constrict in response to inhaled allergens. The most common triggers in cats are dusty litter, scented cleaners or candles, cigarette smoke, and air fresheners. We need to identify and eliminate triggers in the home, plus use inhaled or oral steroids to control inflammation. With proper management most cats live normal lives, but uncontrolled asthma causes permanent lung damage.
preventableNote: Cannot prevent the genetic tendency. Eliminate environmental triggers to reduce flares.
prognosis: Excellent with proper management. Uncontrolled → progressive airway remodeling.
prevention: Low-dust unscented litter (paper, pine). NO scented candles / aerosol sprays / perfume / smoke in cat's environment. HEPA filter. Annual chest radiograph in known asthmatic.

name: Chronic Gingivostomatitis (Cat)
cause: Severe immune-mediated inflammation of the entire oral cavity. Cats appear to mount an overwhelming inflammatory response to the bacterial plaque on their own teeth. Many affected cats are FCV (calicivirus) carriers; some have concurrent FIV or FeLV. Causes intense pain, drooling, weight loss, and reluctance to eat hard food. Often unresponsive to medical therapy alone.
script: This is one of the most painful conditions cats can have — the immune system massively overreacts to even small amounts of plaque on the teeth, causing severe inflammation throughout the mouth. Anti-inflammatory medications help temporarily but don't cure it. The best treatment, in most cases, is full-mouth extraction or extraction of all teeth behind the canines. It sounds drastic, but about 70% of cats are completely cured by it and eat normally even without teeth.
preventableNote: Immune-mediated. Cannot prevent.
prognosis: Medical management: variable, often inadequate. Full-mouth extraction: 70% cured, 20% improved, 10% no response.
prevention: Not preventable. Diagnose early. Plaque-control measures + extraction (often full-mouth) for refractory cases.

name: Feline IBD / Triaditis
cause: Chronic inflammation of the GI tract, sometimes accompanied by simultaneous inflammation of the liver (cholangitis) and pancreas (pancreatitis) — the "triaditis." Suspected to be a combination of dysregulated immune response to gut bacteria + food antigens + genetics. Distinct from intestinal lymphoma but they look similar — biopsy needed to distinguish. Common in middle-aged to older cats.
script: IBD in cats is chronic immune-mediated inflammation of the gut — it's the most common cause of long-term vomiting and diarrhea in middle-aged cats. Often the liver and pancreas are inflamed too, which we call "triaditis." Unfortunately the early signs of IBD and intestinal lymphoma are nearly identical, so we often need biopsies to tell them apart. Treatment is hypoallergenic diet + immunosuppression (usually steroids) — most cats respond well long-term.
preventableNote: Not clearly preventable. Hypoallergenic diet may reduce flares.
prognosis: Good control with diet + immunosuppression. Some progress to lymphoma over years.
prevention: Annual exam + bloodwork to catch early. Hypoallergenic or novel-protein diet trial in any cat with chronic vomiting/diarrhea.

name: Dysbiosis / Fermentation Failure
cause: Hindgut fermenters depend on a delicate balance of beneficial bacteria in the cecum to digest fiber. Anything that disrupts this balance — inappropriate antibiotic (PLACE rule), high-carb/low-fiber diet, sudden diet change, stress, or other illness — allows pathogenic bacteria (especially *Clostridium spiroforme*) to overgrow and produce iota toxin. This causes secretory diarrhea, hemorrhagic typhlitis, and septicemia — the "enterotoxemia" pathway. Often the underlying cause of GI stasis episodes.
script: Fermentation failure means the good bacteria in the gut have been thrown out of balance and the bad ones are taking over. In rabbits and guinea pigs the gut basically runs on these bacteria — when they're disrupted, the wrong species produce toxins that cause severe diarrhea, gas, and can be fatal within a day or two. The most common triggers are the wrong antibiotic, a sudden food change, or eating too much sweet/starchy food. Treatment is fluids, pain control, prokinetics, cholestyramine to bind the toxins, and probiotics to rebuild the flora.
preventableNote: Avoid PLACE-rule antibiotics. Make any diet change gradually over 2 weeks. Probiotic during/after any antibiotic course.
prognosis: Caught early: good with cholestyramine + supportive care. Once septic / hemorrhagic typhlitis: guarded.
prevention: Hay-first diet (no sudden changes). NO oral penicillin/clindamycin/cephalosporin/macrolide. Probiotic (Bene-Bac, fresh cecotrophs from healthy rabbit) during any antibiotic course. Limit pellets + treats.

name: Trichobezoar (Hairballs)
cause: Rabbits ingest their own fur during grooming, especially during shed cycles. Unlike cats, rabbits cannot vomit — swallowed hair must pass through the gut. With normal gut motility + adequate hydration + dietary fiber, hair passes harmlessly in the cecotrophs. When gut motility slows (low-fiber diet, dehydration, stress, dental pain), hair accumulates and matts with food into a solid trichobezoar that blocks the pylorus. **Important: hairballs are usually a *consequence* of slowed gut motility, not the original cause** — finding a trichobezoar means addressing why the gut slowed down first.
script: Rabbits constantly swallow their own fur when grooming — normally it passes through without issue if their gut is moving well. The hairball isn't actually the original problem — it's a sign that the gut had slowed down enough for hair to accumulate. So we need to treat the trichobezoar (fluids, pain meds, motility drugs, sometimes a syringe of pineapple/papaya juice for the enzyme effect) AND figure out why the gut slowed down — diet, dental, dehydration, stress, or hidden pain. Surgery for trichobezoar is a last resort because mortality is high.
preventableNote: Maintain hay-first diet + hydration + regular grooming during shed. Address dental + pain issues early.
prognosis: Medical management: 70-80% resolve. Surgery for obstruction: guarded, 50% mortality.
prevention: Hay-first diet (70%+). Brush daily during shed cycles. Adequate water (wet greens, water bowl AND bottle). Treat dental disease + arthritis. Pineapple/papaya juice during shed (anecdotal — fresh enzymes, not canned).

name: Proventricular Dilatation Disease (PDD)
cause: Caused by Avian Bornavirus (ABV) — a virus that attacks the autonomic nerves controlling the proventriculus (glandular stomach) and ventriculus (gizzard). The nerves stop signaling muscle contraction, so the stomach dilates and food doesn't move through. Birds present with weight loss, regurgitation, undigested seeds in droppings, and sometimes CNS signs (head tremors, seizures, ataxia). Highly contagious between birds via faeces; long incubation period (months to years) makes containment difficult. **No cure.**
script: PDD is caused by a virus called Avian Bornavirus that destroys the nerves controlling the stomach. Without nerve signals, the stomach can't contract properly — food sits and ferments, and you see undigested seeds in the droppings and weight loss despite eating. Sometimes the brain is affected too with tremors or seizures. There's no cure. We can support with easily-digested soft food and anti-inflammatory drugs (celecoxib), which can give months to years of decent quality of life. The hard part is that any other birds in the house have likely already been exposed and should be screened.
preventableNote: Quarantine + ABV PCR screening of new birds (cloacal + crop swab). Long quarantine (90+ days) recommended.
prognosis: No cure. Median survival 6-12 months from diagnosis; some birds live 2-3 years with supportive care.
prevention: 90+ day quarantine for any new bird, with serial ABV PCR. Screen breeding birds. Don't add new birds to a known-positive collection. Disinfect with strong virucide (most don't kill ABV — F10 SC, Virkon).

name: GI Impaction (Reptile)
cause: Loose substrate (sand, calcium sand, crushed walnut, gravel) eaten while hunting insects accumulates in the GI tract. Worsened by: low enclosure temperature (slow motility), dehydration, calcium deficiency / MBD (weak gut muscles can't propel content), large prey items, or fibrous material (coco-fiber). Impaction causes anorexia, straining without defecation, abdominal distention, and eventually obstruction. Most common in juvenile bearded dragons on calcium sand and adult tortoises eating large fibrous items.
script: Impaction in reptiles is almost always a husbandry problem — loose substrate like sand or calcium sand gets swallowed during feeding and builds up faster than the gut can move it out. The problem gets dramatically worse if the enclosure is too cold (their gut slows when cold) or if calcium is low (the gut muscles can't contract well). We'll start with warm soaks, increased temperature, fluids, and a mild laxative; surgery is sometimes needed for severe impactions. Going forward: NO loose substrate — use reptile carpet, tile, or paper instead.
preventableNote: 100% preventable with solid substrate (tile, paper, reptile carpet) + correct POTZ + calcium supplementation.
prognosis: Mild: resolves with soaks + warmth + laxative. Severe: surgery, guarded.
prevention: NEVER loose particulate substrate (sand, calcium sand, crushed walnut, gravel). Use ceramic tile, reptile carpet, paper towel, or newspaper. Correct POTZ. Calcium dust on every meal for juveniles. Prey size ≤ space between the eyes.

name: Ear Mites — Rabbit (Psoroptes)
cause: *Psoroptes cuniculi* is a non-burrowing ear mite that lives in the ear canal of rabbits. Causes severe irritation, thick brown/yellow crusts that fill the canal and can extend onto the face and neck, head shaking, ear scratching, and sometimes secondary otitis media with head tilt. Highly contagious between rabbits. Mites also affect guinea pigs occasionally.
script: Ear mites in rabbits make these thick crumbly brown crusts you can see filling the ear canal. They itch terribly. The good news is that treatment is very effective — a single injection or two doses of selamectin/ivermectin clears the mites completely, and the crusts will slowly come off on their own. Don't pick them out manually — that's very painful and can damage the ear canal. Any other rabbits at home should also be treated since the mites are highly contagious.
preventableNote: Quarantine new rabbits 30 days. Inspect ears at every vet visit.
prognosis: Excellent with ivermectin or selamectin × 2-3 doses.
prevention: Quarantine new rabbits + check ears. Treat all in-contact rabbits simultaneously. Disinfect environment + bedding.

name: Ear Base Abscess
also: Aural abscess, otitis media-interna, retrobulbar abscess
cause: A walled-off pocket of pus deep in/behind the ear canal. **Rabbit**: usually *Pasteurella multocida* ascending from the nasopharynx or extending from a tooth root abscess into the bulla and ear base; rabbit pus is thick, caseous (toothpaste consistency) and does NOT drain through a needle — surgical marsupialization required. Often accompanied by otitis media-interna → head tilt, facial nerve paresis, anorexia. **Reptile (especially chelonian)**: classic presentation of **hypovitaminosis A** — squamous metaplasia of the auditory tube allows secondary bacterial infection to wall off into the tympanic cavity; presents as visible mass behind the tympanic membrane. **Bird**: less common, usually from chronic sinusitis extending to peri-aural tissues, or from systemic mycobacteriosis. All forms cause significant PAIN → leading to anorexia (a key early-warning sign) and reluctance to handle the head.
script: That lump near the ear is a deep pocket of infection that has walled itself off — what we call an ear-base abscess. In rabbits, the pus is so thick (like toothpaste) that we can't just drain it with a needle — it has to be opened surgically and packed open so the body can heal from the inside out. The infection has probably been brewing for weeks or months by the time you noticed it — it commonly comes from the nose or a tooth root extending in. Beyond the lump, the bigger concern is that this is **painful**, which is why she stopped eating. We need to get pain control on board first, then plan culture-guided antibiotics for several weeks, and surgery soon.
preventableNote: Rabbit: aggressive dental disease management; control nasal Pasteurella. Reptile: lifelong vitamin A through varied diet (dark leafy greens). Treat upper respiratory infections early before they migrate.
prognosis: Rabbit: guarded — recurrence common; long-term antibiotics (weeks–months); facial nerve deficits and head tilt may persist even with successful treatment. Reptile: good with surgical evacuation + dietary correction; usually one-time event if vitamin A normalized. Bird: variable; treat underlying systemic disease.
prevention: Rabbit: hay-first diet + 6-monthly dental check + treat sneezes promptly. Reptile: dark leafy greens + occasional egg yolk for omnivores; avoid iceberg-lettuce-only diets. Bird: address chronic sinusitis early.

name: Dacryocystitis (Rabbit)
also: Tear duct infection, chronic ocular discharge rabbit
cause: The rabbit nasolacrimal duct passes right next to the roots of the upper incisors and cheek teeth. When these tooth roots elongate or become infected (a frequent occurrence — see dental disease), they compress and block the duct, and bacteria ascend from the nose/mouth into the lacrimal sac. The result is chronic milky-white discharge from one or both eyes, with no actual eye disease underneath — the eye itself is normal; the plumbing is broken.
script: That milky white discharge from her eye looks like an eye infection, but the eye itself is actually fine — the problem is the tear duct. It runs right next to the roots of her teeth, and when the teeth grow into the wrong place (which is extremely common in rabbits), they squeeze the duct closed and infection backs up. So we can flush the duct and give antibiotics, but the only real fix is dealing with the underlying dental disease — which means radiographs and possibly extractions. Otherwise it just keeps coming back.
preventableNote: Hay-first diet from young age reduces dental disease risk.
prognosis: Symptomatic management: variable, often recurrent. Definitive treatment requires addressing dental root cause.
prevention: Hay-first diet. Annual dental + skull radiograph in middle-aged + lop breed rabbits. Treat any tooth root pathology early.

name: Egg Yolk Peritonitis (Bird)
cause: During ovulation, the ovary releases an egg that should be caught by the infundibulum of the oviduct. When the egg misses (due to chronic egg-laying, oviduct disease, stress, or pre-existing peritonitis), the yolk falls into the coelomic cavity. Yolk is highly inflammatory and rich in nutrients for bacteria — within hours the bird is septic. Common in chronic-laying budgies, cockatiels, lovebirds, and backyard chickens.
script: Egg yolk peritonitis is when an egg yolk ends up in the body cavity instead of inside the oviduct where it belongs. The yolk is full of fat and protein — bacteria multiply explosively, and within hours she's septic. This is a real emergency. We need IV fluids, antibiotics, supportive care, and often surgery to drain and flush the coelom. Going forward, we need to stop the chronic egg-laying with hormone implants (deslorelin) — otherwise this will recur.
preventableNote: Stop chronic egg laying: no nest sites, < 10h photoperiod, remove "mate" perception (mirrors), deslorelin implant.
prognosis: Caught early + aggressive treatment: 60-70% survival. Septic shock: guarded to grave.
prevention: For chronic-laying hens: deslorelin implant. NO mirrors / nest boxes / dark hiding spots for single hens. Reduce photoperiod < 10h/day. Pellet diet (calcium-balanced).

name: Dysecdysis (Incomplete Shed)
cause: Shedding failure due to inadequate humidity (most common cause), poor nutrition (vitamin A, hypovitaminosis), mite infestation, dehydration, or no suitable rough surface to rub against. Retained shed around toes constricts circulation and causes toe loss; retained eye caps (spectacle) over snake eyes can cause blindness; retained tail tip shed causes necrosis and amputation.
script: Incomplete shedding in reptiles is almost always a humidity problem — the old skin needs to be hydrated to slough off properly. In snakes especially, the retained eye cap can be a real problem because it blocks vision and predisposes to infection. We'll do warm soaks today to soften the retained skin and gently peel it off (never pull dry shed — it can rip the new skin underneath). Going forward, providing a humid hide and a slightly higher overall humidity during shed cycles prevents recurrence.
preventableNote: Provide humid hide, correct humidity for species, rough rubbing surface, good nutrition.
prognosis: Excellent if treated before tissue necrosis. Lost toe / tail tip: permanent.
prevention: Humid hide (damp moss/paper) always available. Increase ambient humidity during pre-shed (blue-eye phase). Rough surface (cork bark, rocks) to rub against. Annual vitamin A check in tortoises/turtles.

name: Guinea Pig Pneumonia (Bordetella / Strep)
cause: *Bordetella bronchiseptica* (the same kennel-cough organism) and *Streptococcus pneumoniae* are the two top causes of pneumonia in guinea pigs. Often introduced by direct or fomite contact with infected dogs, rabbits, or other guinea pigs (many of which carry it asymptomatically). Stress, vitamin C deficiency, and concurrent disease are the typical triggers. Highly contagious within a guinea pig colony — can wipe out groups quickly.
script: Guinea pig pneumonia is most commonly caused by Bordetella — the same bacteria as kennel cough in dogs. Many dogs and rabbits carry it without signs but can pass it to guinea pigs, who get severely ill. Stress, low vitamin C, and concurrent illness make it much worse. We'll start antibiotics (TMS or enrofloxacin — never amoxicillin), oxygen support, and high-dose vitamin C. Isolate from any other guinea pigs in the home, and keep dogs and rabbits away from her recovery area.
preventableNote: No vaccine. Reduce risk: keep separate from dogs and rabbits, quarantine new pigs, adequate vitamin C, stress reduction.
prognosis: Caught early: good with antibiotics. Severe (cyanosis, hypothermia): guarded.
prevention: Don't house with rabbits or expose to dogs. Quarantine new pigs 30 days. Daily Vit C. Maintain good husbandry + low stress.

name: Corneal Ulcer (Ulcerative Keratitis)
cause: A break in the cornea's surface layer (epithelium) exposing the deeper stroma. Common causes: trauma or a scratch (cat claw, hay, sand), a foreign body trapped under the eyelid, inturned eyelids or abnormal lashes, dry eye (insufficient tear film), reduced blink in bulgy-eyed brachycephalic breeds, and secondary bacterial infection that deepens the ulcer. In rabbits and guinea pigs, hay/bedding fragments and dental-related tear-duct disease are frequent triggers. Deep or infected ulcers can melt and perforate the eye within 24-48h.
script: An ulcer is a scratch or sore on the clear front window of the eye, called the cornea. It is very painful — that is why the eye is held shut, watering, and red. Most start from a scratch, a bit of hay or grit caught under the lid, or a dry eye that does not lubricate properly. We put a harmless orange dye in the eye to show exactly where the ulcer is. Simple surface scratches heal in a few days with eye drops, but if it is deep or infected it can threaten the whole eye, so we treat these seriously and recheck closely.
preventableNote: Trauma is hard to prevent. Many cases are preventable by treating dry eye, removing abnormal lashes, and keeping bedding/hay dust low.
prognosis: Superficial ulcers heal in 3-7 days. Deep, melting, or infected ulcers are guarded and may need surgery (e.g. conjunctival graft) to save the eye.
prevention: Treat underlying dry eye and eyelid abnormalities. Use low-dust bedding for small mammals. Protect eyes during play. NEVER use steroid eye drops on an undiagnosed red, painful eye — they make ulcers worse.

name: Conjunctivitis
cause: Inflammation of the conjunctiva (the pink membrane lining the lids and white of the eye). Causes vary by species: in cats, feline herpesvirus and Chlamydia are top infectious causes; in rabbits and guinea pigs it is often bacterial (Pasteurella, Bordetella, Staphylococcus) or secondary to dental tear-duct disease and bedding irritants; in birds it accompanies respiratory infection (Chlamydia/psittacosis, mycoplasma) or hypovitaminosis A. Allergens, dust, and ammonia from soiled enclosures are common non-infectious triggers.
script: Conjunctivitis means the pink lining around the eye is inflamed — red, puffy, and producing discharge. It can be from an infection, an irritant like dust or ammonia from bedding, an allergy, or a blocked tear duct. In cats it is very often a herpes or chlamydia infection. It is usually not serious on its own, but we check that the cornea underneath is not ulcerated and look for an underlying cause, because treating only the surface lets it keep coming back.
preventableNote: Reduce dust, ammonia, and overcrowding. Cat herpesvirus is lifelong once acquired and flares with stress, so it is managed rather than cured.
prognosis: Most cases resolve in 1-2 weeks with treatment of the cause. Herpesviral and chlamydial cases in cats tend to recur.
prevention: Clean enclosures and good ventilation (low ammonia). Reduce stress for herpes-positive cats. Vaccinate cats (FVRCP). Correct vitamin A deficiency in birds and reptiles.

name: Glaucoma
cause: Pressure inside the eye rises because fluid (aqueous humour) cannot drain out fast enough. Primary glaucoma is an inherited drainage-angle defect seen in certain dog breeds; secondary glaucoma follows another eye problem that blocks drainage — chronic inflammation (uveitis), a lens that has shifted position, bleeding inside the eye, or a tumour. The high pressure rapidly damages the retina and optic nerve, and a sustained spike can cause permanent blindness within 24-48 hours. Over time the whole eye stretches and enlarges (buphthalmos).
script: Glaucoma means the pressure inside the eye is dangerously high because the fluid that normally drains away is trapped. It is painful — like a constant pounding headache behind the eye — and it can destroy vision within a day or two if we do not bring the pressure down fast. That is why a red, cloudy, enlarged, or suddenly blind eye is an emergency. We measure the pressure with a special instrument and start medication immediately to protect any remaining sight.
preventableNote: Primary (inherited) glaucoma cannot be prevented, but screening at-risk breeds and treating the other eye early helps. Secondary glaucoma is reduced by promptly treating uveitis and lens problems.
prognosis: Vision loss is often permanent if pressure is not controlled within hours. Many chronically painful blind eyes ultimately need removal or other surgery for comfort.
prevention: Breed screening (gonioscopy) in predisposed dogs. Treat uveitis, lens luxation, and intraocular bleeding promptly. Prophylactic drops in the fellow eye for primary glaucoma.

name: Anterior Uveitis
cause: Inflammation inside the front chamber of the eye (iris and ciliary body). It can follow trauma or a deep corneal ulcer, but is very often a sign of disease elsewhere in the body: infections (in cats FIP, FeLV, FIV, toxoplasma; in rabbits Encephalitozoon cuniculi causing lens rupture and phacoclastic uveitis), tick-borne disease, immune-mediated disease, high blood pressure, or cancer. Signs include a cloudy or red eye, squinting, a small pupil, and material settling in the front chamber. If uncontrolled it leads to glaucoma, cataract, and blindness.
script: Uveitis is inflammation inside the eye itself — not just on the surface. The eye looks cloudy, red, and painful, and the pupil is often small. The important thing is that uveitis is frequently the eye showing us a problem in the rest of the body — an infection, high blood pressure, or occasionally cancer — so we usually recommend bloodwork and a blood-pressure check, not just eye drops. Controlling the inflammation quickly protects the eye from going on to glaucoma or cataract.
preventableNote: Often secondary to systemic disease, so prevention means controlling those (e.g. tick prevention, managing FeLV/FIV cats, early E. cuniculi treatment in rabbits).
prognosis: Good if the underlying cause is found and treated early. Recurrent or uncontrolled uveitis carries a guarded visual prognosis.
prevention: Tick prevention. Retroviral testing and stress reduction in cats. Prompt treatment of E. cuniculi in rabbits. Regular blood-pressure checks in older cats.

name: Keratoconjunctivitis Sicca (Dry Eye / KCS)
cause: The eye does not produce enough watery tears, so the cornea and conjunctiva dry out, become inflamed, and are prone to ulcers. The most common cause in dogs is immune-mediated destruction of the tear glands; certain breeds are predisposed. Other causes include some drugs (notably sulfonamide antibiotics), removal of the third-eyelid tear gland (cherry eye), nerve damage, distemper, and endocrine disease. The classic sign is a thick, sticky, ropey discharge with a dull, dry-looking, often pigmented cornea.
script: Dry eye means the tear glands are not making enough of the watery part of tears, so the surface is constantly dry and irritated. That is why you see thick gluey discharge and a dull, red eye. In dogs it is usually the immune system mistakenly attacking the tear glands. It is a lifelong condition, but it is very manageable — a tear-stimulating ointment (like ciclosporin) often gets the glands producing tears again, which keeps the eye comfortable and prevents painful ulcers and vision loss.
preventableNote: Immune-mediated cases are not preventable but are very controllable with daily medication. Avoid sulfa drugs in predisposed breeds; surgically replace (do not remove) a prolapsed tear gland.
prognosis: Excellent comfort and vision with lifelong tear-stimulant therapy in most dogs. Untreated dry eye leads to chronic pain, scarring, and blindness.
prevention: Replace rather than excise the third-eyelid gland in cherry eye. Avoid sulfonamide drugs in at-risk dogs. Measure tear production (Schirmer test) in predisposed breeds and any chronic red eye.

name: Cataract
cause: The normally clear lens becomes cloudy, blocking light from reaching the retina. Causes include inheritance (many dog breeds), diabetes mellitus (a very common cause of rapid cataracts in dogs), aging, intraocular inflammation, trauma, and nutritional problems in hand-reared young animals. In rabbits, Encephalitozoon cuniculi infecting the lens is a recognised cause. A rapidly swelling or ruptured cataract can itself trigger painful inflammation (lens-induced uveitis) and secondary glaucoma.
script: A cataract is a clouding of the lens inside the eye — the part that focuses light — so the pupil looks white or grey and vision is reduced. Common causes are genetics, aging, and especially diabetes in dogs, where cataracts can form within weeks. Cataracts themselves are not usually an emergency, but they can leak and inflame the eye, so we monitor them. Vision can often be restored with surgery to remove the cloudy lens, and in diabetics good sugar control is part of the picture.
preventableNote: Inherited and age-related cataracts are not preventable. Diabetic cataracts are reduced by good glycaemic control; E. cuniculi cataracts by treating the parasite.
prognosis: Vision is often restored with cataract surgery in suitable candidates. Untreated, cataracts can cause lens-induced uveitis and glaucoma.
prevention: Screen breeding dogs for hereditary cataract. Tight diabetic control. Treat E. cuniculi in rabbits and intraocular inflammation promptly.

name: Nasolacrimal Duct Obstruction / Epiphora
also: Blocked tear duct, tear overflow, dacryocystitis
cause: Tears normally drain from the eye into the nose through the nasolacrimal duct. When that duct is blocked, tears overflow onto the face (epiphora) and the tear sac can become infected (dacryocystitis), producing milky or pus-like discharge. In rabbits this is extremely common because the duct runs right alongside the roots of the upper incisors and cheek teeth — elongated or infected tooth roots compress and block it, so it is fundamentally a dental problem. In dogs and cats, causes include congenital narrow ducts, brachycephalic facial conformation, infection, foreign material, or scarring.
script: This watery (or milky) overflow down the face is usually a plumbing problem, not a problem with the eye itself. The tiny tube that should drain tears from the eye into the nose is blocked, so the tears spill over the eyelids. In rabbits especially, the blockage almost always comes from the tooth roots, which sit right next to that drainage tube — so the real fix involves checking the teeth, often with X-rays. We can flush the duct to clear it, but unless we address the underlying cause it tends to come back.
preventableNote: In rabbits, a hay-first diet that limits dental disease is the main preventive lever. Congenital and conformational cases in dogs/cats cannot be prevented.
prognosis: Good for comfort with flushing and treating the cause; rabbit cases are frequently recurrent because of the underlying dental disease.
prevention: Hay-first diet and routine dental/skull radiographs in rabbits (especially lop and middle-aged). Flush ducts and treat infection early. Address tooth-root disease at the source.

name: Retrobulbar Abscess / Exophthalmos
also: Orbital abscess, bulging eye, orbital cellulitis
cause: A pocket of infection forms in the orbit behind the eyeball, pushing the eye forward so it bulges (exophthalmos) and often cannot fully close. In rabbits and rodents this is most often an extension of dental disease — an infected upper cheek-tooth root erodes back into the orbit. Other causes include penetrating wounds, foreign bodies, and tumours behind the eye. The exposed, protruding cornea quickly dries and ulcerates, and the infection can be difficult to clear because rabbit pus is thick and walled-off.
script: The eye is bulging because something behind it — most often a pocket of infection from a diseased tooth root — is pushing it forward. It looks dramatic and it is uncomfortable, and because the eye can no longer blink properly the surface starts to dry out and ulcerate. We will image the skull (X-rays or a CT scan) to find the source, because in rabbits this is nearly always linked to the teeth. Treatment combines antibiotics, draining or removing the abscess, and dealing with the offending tooth; sometimes the eye cannot be saved and removal is the kindest option.
preventableNote: Largely tied to dental disease in rabbits/rodents, so a hay-first diet and early dental care reduce risk. Trauma-related cases are not preventable.
prognosis: Guarded — abscesses are notoriously recurrent in rabbits. Outcome is best when the source tooth is removed; advanced cases may require enucleation.
prevention: Hay-first diet and regular dental exams/radiographs in rabbits and rodents. Treat tooth-root infections early. Prevent eye trauma.

name: Corneal Lipidosis (Lipid Keratopathy)
cause: Fat (lipid) deposits accumulate within the cornea, producing a cloudy white or greyish patch. In pet birds it is associated with high-fat (seed-heavy) diets and high blood cholesterol, and is seen relatively often in species such as budgerigars and some raptors and waterfowl. In dogs, lipid can deposit in the cornea with high blood cholesterol or as a localised degeneration. The deposits themselves are usually not painful unless they are extensive enough to disrupt the surface, but they signal an underlying lipid or dietary problem that should be addressed.
script: That cloudy white area on the eye is a deposit of fat within the clear part of the eye. In birds it is usually a sign of a diet that is too high in fat — typically a seed-based diet — and of high cholesterol in the blood. It is not usually painful, but it can blur vision and tells us the diet and metabolism need attention. We will recommend converting to a balanced pelleted diet and may check bloodwork, since correcting the underlying problem is the main way to keep it from worsening.
preventableNote: In birds, largely preventable/limited by feeding a balanced pelleted diet instead of high-fat seed mixes. Some dog cases are degenerative and not diet-driven.
prognosis: Deposits often persist but are slow to progress once the diet/lipid problem is corrected; vision is usually preserved unless deposits are extensive.
prevention: Convert seed-eating birds to a balanced formulated/pelleted diet; limit fatty seeds (sunflower, millet). Screen and manage high cholesterol where relevant.

name: Hyphema
cause: Blood collects in the front chamber of the eye, in front of the iris. It is a sign rather than a disease in itself: causes include trauma (a blow to the eye), severe intraocular inflammation (uveitis), high blood pressure (common in older cats and dogs with kidney or thyroid disease), clotting disorders and poisoning (e.g. rodenticide), retinal detachment, and tumours inside the eye. Depending on the cause, the bleeding may clear on its own or signal a serious systemic problem, and the blood can also raise eye pressure and cause secondary glaucoma.
script: There is blood pooling inside the front of the eye. This is important because, apart from a direct injury, it is often a warning sign of a body-wide problem — most commonly high blood pressure, but also clotting problems, poisoning, or a tumour. So besides treating the eye, we usually recommend checking blood pressure and bloodwork to find why it bled. We also watch the eye pressure closely, because trapped blood can push the pressure up and threaten vision.
preventableNote: Trauma is hard to prevent; many medical causes are reduced by controlling blood pressure, keeping rodenticides away, and monitoring older pets.
prognosis: Variable — traumatic hyphema in an otherwise healthy eye often clears. Hyphema from hypertension, clotting disease, or tumour carries a guarded prognosis tied to the underlying cause.
prevention: Control blood pressure in older cats/dogs; screen for kidney/thyroid disease. Keep rodenticides inaccessible. Protect eyes from trauma.

name: Intestinal Obstruction / Acute Gastric Dilation
cause: A true blockage of the gut — most often a felted ball of fur (compacted at a narrow point of the small intestine), but also pellets of dried food, carpet fibres, or a locust-bean/dried-fruit treat that swells. Gas and fluid build up behind the blockage, so the stomach balloons (gastric dilation) within hours and the rabbit goes into pain and shock. This is the single most important emergency to tell apart from simple GI stasis, because the treatment is the opposite — an obstructed, gas-filled stomach can RUPTURE if you give gut-motility drugs or massage the belly.
script: This is different from the slow-gut problem (stasis) — here something is physically blocking the intestine, and gas is rapidly blowing up the stomach like a balloon. It is intensely painful and an emergency. We will take an X-ray right away: a hugely distended, gas-filled stomach with an empty gut behind it points to a blockage. The danger is that the usual stasis drugs that speed the gut up can burst an obstructed stomach, so we must confirm the picture first. We stabilise with pain relief and fluids and decompress the stomach; some cases need emergency surgery.
preventableNote: Reduce fur ingestion (grooming, hay), avoid swelling treats (dried fruit, locust beans), low-carpet-fibre environment. Some are unavoidable.
prognosis: Guarded — a surgical emergency. Good if decompressed/operated early; gastric rupture is rapidly fatal.
prevention: Plenty of hay + grooming to limit fur balls. Avoid dried fruit / locust-bean / bread treats that swell. ⚠ DO NOT give prokinetics or abdominal massage until obstruction is ruled out (no caecal gas + huge gastric gas = obstruction).

name: Myxomatosis
cause: A pox-family virus (myxoma virus) spread mainly by biting insects — fleas and mosquitoes — and by direct contact. The classic form causes swelling of the eyelids, lips, ears and genitals (the puffy "big head" look), thick eye discharge that glues the lids shut, fever, and difficulty breathing. In unvaccinated pet rabbits it is usually fatal over 1–2 weeks. A vaccine is available and is the main protection.
script: Myxomatosis is a serious viral disease spread by mosquito and flea bites. The swelling around the eyes, ears and bottom and the gummy eyes are typical. Sadly, in an unvaccinated rabbit it is very often fatal, and treatment is mostly supportive — fluids, pain relief, keeping the eyes clean and the rabbit eating. The most important message is prevention: vaccination plus insect control (mosquito screens, flea treatment) is how we keep rabbits safe.
preventableNote: Vaccine-preventable (combined RHD/myxomatosis vaccines where available) + flea/mosquito control.
prognosis: Grave in unvaccinated rabbits — most die or are euthanased. Vaccinated rabbits may get a milder, survivable form.
prevention: Vaccinate (myxomatosis + RHDV). Mosquito netting on hutches, flea prevention, avoid contact with wild rabbits.

name: Pregnancy Toxaemia (Rabbit)
cause: A dangerous fat-and-sugar crisis in late pregnancy, just after kindling, or in a false (pseudo) pregnancy — especially in overweight does or any doe that suddenly stops eating. With too little energy coming in, the body breaks down fat so fast that the liver becomes overwhelmed (hepatic lipidosis) and acidic ketones build up in the blood. The doe becomes weak, dull, and may collapse, seizure, and die quickly if not treated.
script: This is an energy emergency seen in late-pregnant, just-delivered, or falsely-pregnant rabbits — particularly if they are overweight or have gone off their food. When a rabbit stops eating, the body burns fat so fast that the liver clogs up and the blood turns acidic. It can progress to collapse very quickly, so we treat aggressively with sugar/fluids, force-feeding, and liver support. Prevention is mostly about never letting a pregnant doe become obese or skip meals.
preventableNote: Avoid obesity in breeding does; never let a pregnant/post-kindling doe go anorexic; ensure constant access to food + reduce stress.
prognosis: Guarded — high mortality once collapsed. Better if caught at the early "off food + dull" stage.
prevention: Keep breeding does lean and well-fed; constant hay/greens; minimise stress around kindling; treat any anorexia in a pregnant doe as urgent.

name: Thymoma
cause: A tumour of the thymus gland in the front of the chest. As it grows it takes up room the lungs need and presses on the big veins returning blood to the heart, so the rabbit becomes breathless and tires easily. A very characteristic sign is BOTH eyes bulging (bilateral exophthalmos), worse when the head is lowered, because blood cannot drain from the head. Some rabbits also get a flaky skin condition as a side-effect of the tumour. In ferrets the thymic/lymphoid form is part of their lymphoma spectrum.
script: There is a mass in the front of the chest, in the thymus gland. Because it crowds the lungs and squeezes the big veins, you may see laboured breathing and — very typically — both eyes bulging, especially when the head goes down. We confirm it with chest X-rays or an ultrasound/CT. The good news is that, unlike many chest tumours, thymoma can often be treated — with radiation therapy or surgery — so it is worth investigating properly rather than assuming the worst.
preventableNote: Not preventable. Early detection (investigating breathlessness or bulging eyes) gives the best treatment options.
prognosis: Fair to good with radiation therapy or surgery; guarded if left untreated (progressive respiratory failure).
prevention: None. Investigate any rabbit with chronic breathlessness or both eyes bulging.

name: Otitis Media / Interna (Rabbit)
cause: Infection deep in the ear — behind the eardrum (middle ear) or in the balance organ (inner ear). It usually arrives by one of two routes: bacteria such as Pasteurella travelling up the eustachian tube from the nose/throat, or — in lop-eared rabbits especially — a build-up of wax and debris in a narrow ear canal. The result is a head tilt, loss of balance, rolling, and rapid flicking eyes. It looks identical to E. cuniculi, which is the main thing it must be told apart from.
script: Your rabbit's head tilt and loss of balance come from an infection deep inside the ear, in the part that controls balance. The two big causes of a head tilt in rabbits are this ear infection and a parasite called E. cuniculi, and they look the same from the outside — so we often image the skull (X-ray or ideally CT) and may need long courses of antibiotics. With patience many rabbits improve, though a residual head tilt can remain. Deep or stubborn infections sometimes need surgery to drain the ear.
preventableNote: Treat upper-respiratory (Pasteurella) infections early; routine ear checks in lop breeds. Genetics (lop conformation) is not preventable.
prognosis: Fair — many improve with prolonged antibiotics; a permanent head tilt may persist. Severe cases may need bulla surgery.
prevention: Early treatment of snuffles/URI; regular ear cleaning checks in lop-eared rabbits; reduce stress.

name: Cardiomyopathy / Heart Failure (Rabbit)
cause: Disease of the heart muscle — either a thinned, weakly-pumping heart (dilated) or a thickened, stiff one (hypertrophic). It is increasingly recognised in older and giant-breed rabbits and is often silent until the rabbit suddenly struggles to breathe (fluid backing up into the lungs) or tires very easily. Stress and the catecholamine surge it causes can damage the rabbit heart, and giving aggressive intravenous fluids to a rabbit with hidden heart disease can tip it into heart failure.
script: Your rabbit's heart muscle is not working as it should, and fluid is backing up — that is why the breathing is fast or laboured and energy is low. We confirm with an ultrasound of the heart and chest X-rays. We can help a lot with heart medications (such as a diuretic and a heart-support drug), but rabbits hide heart disease well, so we are careful with fluids and stress, both of which can push a weak rabbit heart over the edge. Keeping handling calm is part of the treatment.
preventableNote: Largely age/genetic. Minimise stress; be cautious with IV fluid rates in older rabbits; screen giant breeds.
prognosis: Guarded — manageable for a time with medication once in failure; sudden death possible.
prevention: Low-stress handling; cautious fluid therapy in older rabbits; investigate early exercise intolerance or fast breathing.

name: Aortic / Soft-tissue Mineralization (Rabbit)
cause: Calcium is laid down in the walls of the aorta and other soft tissues, making the great vessels stiff. Rabbits absorb dietary calcium very efficiently and excrete the excess in urine, so a calcium- or vitamin-D-excess diet, or chronic kidney disease, predisposes them. It is common in some lines (reported in a high proportion of certain laboratory rabbits) and is usually found as an incidental "chalky aorta" on X-ray, but heavy mineralization reduces cardiac efficiency and can contribute to sudden death.
script: The walls of the big blood vessels have hardened with calcium deposits — a bit like limescale in a pipe. In rabbits this is linked to too much calcium or vitamin D in the diet and to kidney problems, because rabbits handle calcium very differently from cats and dogs. Often we find it by chance on an X-ray. We can't reverse the deposits, but we can slow things down by correcting the diet (the right amount of calcium, mostly grass hay) and checking the kidneys.
preventableNote: Avoid calcium/vitamin-D-excess diets (limit alfalfa + supplements in adults); manage kidney disease.
prognosis: Chronic; often incidental. Heavy mineralization reduces cardiac reserve and is linked to sudden death.
prevention: Appropriate adult diet (grass hay-based, limit alfalfa and calcium-rich pellets/supplements); monitor renal function.

name: Chronic Kidney Disease (Rabbit)
cause: Slow, progressive loss of kidney function in older rabbits — from previous E. cuniculi infection, chronic urinary "sludge"/stones, high-calcium diets and tissue mineralization, or simple age-related scarring. Importantly, blood urea and creatinine are insensitive in rabbits, so a rabbit can have significant kidney disease with near-normal routine bloods; urine concentration and the urine protein:creatinine ratio are more useful.
script: Your rabbit's kidneys are gradually wearing out, which is why you may see more drinking and urinating, weight loss, and a poor coat.
