A tricky point in rabbits is that the usual kidney blood tests can look almost normal even when the kidneys are quite damaged, so we lean on urine tests and sometimes imaging. We can't cure it, but with the right diet, good hydration, and treating any underlying cause like E. cuniculi we can often keep them comfortable for a long time.
preventableNote: Treat E. cuniculi and urinary sludge early; appropriate (not excessive) dietary calcium; maintain hydration.
prognosis: Chronic and progressive; many rabbits live well for months–years with diet and supportive care.
prevention: Correct adult diet (limit excess calcium), encourage water intake, treat E. cuniculi/urolithiasis early, monitor older rabbits.

name: Cystitis / Hypercalciuria / Urine Scald (Rabbit)
cause: Because rabbits excrete excess calcium in their urine, it can turn thick and pasty ("bladder sludge") and irritate or infect the bladder. High-calcium diets, too little water, obesity, pain or arthritis (so the rabbit cannot posture to empty fully), and a dirty environment all contribute. Affected rabbits strain, pass blood-tinged or chalky urine, and the constant dribbling scalds the skin of the inner thighs and bottom (urine scald), which is painful and attracts flies.
script: Your rabbit's urine has become thick and irritating — partly because rabbits naturally dump extra calcium into their urine — and it is inflaming the bladder and scalding the skin underneath. That is why you see straining, blood-tinged or gritty urine, and a sore, wet bottom. We will check for sludge or stones (often with an X-ray), flush the bladder if needed, give pain relief, and treat the skin. Long-term we focus on more water, the right diet, weight loss, and treating any arthritis that stops them posturing properly.
preventableNote: Encourage water intake, appropriate (not high) calcium diet, weight control, clean dry housing, treat underlying arthritis/pain.
prognosis: Good for comfort with treatment; tends to recur unless diet, hydration and mobility are addressed.
prevention: Plenty of water (multiple sources), grass-hay diet limiting high-calcium pellets/alfalfa, keep rabbit lean and mobile, clean litter.

name: Mastitis (Rabbit)
cause: Inflammation/infection of the mammary glands, usually in nursing or falsely-pregnant does. A septic form (Pasteurella, Staphylococcus, Streptococcus) makes the glands hot, hard, red/blue and painful with fever and a very sick rabbit, and can progress to blood poisoning. A separate cystic mastitis is seen in older intact does and is often linked to underlying uterine disease rather than infection.
script: The milk glands are infected and inflamed — hot, swollen and painful — and your rabbit has a fever and feels very unwell. This needs prompt antibiotics, pain relief, and supportive care because it can progress to blood poisoning. If kits are nursing we manage them carefully. In an older, non-breeding rabbit, lumpy mammary glands can instead signal uterine disease, so we usually recommend checking/ spaying. Spaying prevents both the uterine disease and many of these mammary problems.
preventableNote: Good nest hygiene in breeding does; spaying prevents the cystic/uterine-linked form and reduces risk.
prognosis: Good if treated early; guarded if septic/gangrenous with systemic illness.
prevention: Hygienic kindling environment, avoid trauma to glands, spay non-breeding does (also prevents uterine adenocarcinoma).

name: Rabbit Syphilis (Treponematosis)
cause: A bacterial infection (Treponema paraluiscuniculi) — a relative of the syphilis organism but specific to rabbits and NOT transmissible to humans. It is spread by mating and from doe to kit at birth. It causes crusty, scabby, sometimes ulcerated lesions around the genitals, anus, nose, lips and eyelids. Many rabbits carry it silently and flare under stress. It responds very well to injectable penicillin (never oral in rabbits).
script: These crusty sores around the nose, lips, or genitals are caused by a rabbit-specific bacterium — it is sometimes called rabbit syphilis, but rest assured it does NOT spread to people. It is passed by mating and from mother to babies. The good news is it treats well with a course of penicillin injections (we never give penicillin by mouth to rabbits — that is dangerous). Any in-contact or breeding partners should be treated too.
preventableNote: Screen/treat breeding stock; treat in-contact rabbits; reduce stress that triggers flares.
prognosis: Excellent with parenteral penicillin; lesions resolve over a few weeks.
prevention: Test and treat breeding rabbits and their mates; quarantine new rabbits; treat the doe before breeding to protect kits.

name: Cheyletiellosis (Walking Dandruff)
cause: A surface fur mite (Cheyletiella parasitovorax) that lives in the coat and produces large, flaky scales along the back and neck that appear to move ("walking dandruff"). Itching is usually mild. Heavy infestations often signal that the rabbit cannot groom properly — because of obesity, dental pain, arthritis, or another illness — so it is also a marker of underlying disease. It is ZOONOTIC, causing a temporary itchy rash on people who handle affected pets.
script: Those big flakes of dandruff over the back are caused by a fur mite — you may even see the scale "move", which is the mites. The itch is usually mild. An important clue is that a heavy case often means the rabbit cannot groom itself well, so we also check for hidden problems like dental disease, arthritis or obesity. It is easily treated (e.g. with selamectin or ivermectin), and a quick note — it can give people a temporary itchy rash, so we treat it promptly.
preventableNote: Treat underlying problems that impair grooming (obesity, dental disease, arthritis); quarantine new animals.
prognosis: Excellent — responds well to selamectin/ivermectin (avoid fipronil in rabbits).
prevention: Quarantine + check new rabbits; address grooming-impairing illness; treat in-contact animals and environment.

name: Mucoid Enteropathy (Rabbit)
cause: A distinct disturbance of the rabbit hindgut — not a simple infection — in which the caecum and colon stop moving normally and the colon pours out thick gelatinous mucus. It is most common in weanling/juvenile rabbits and is linked to low-fibre diets, stress, dysbiosis, and disordered gut motility. Rabbits pass mucus-covered or jelly-like faeces, bloat, grind their teeth in pain, and can become impacted.
script: Your rabbit has a hindgut problem where the lower bowel has stopped moving properly and is producing thick jelly-like mucus — you may see mucus around the droppings. It is different from a normal infection and is strongly tied to not enough fibre, stress, and an upset in the gut bacteria, especially in young rabbits. Treatment focuses on getting fibre and gut movement going again — fluids, pain relief, encouraging hay, and supportive feeding — while we rule out a blockage.
preventableNote: High-fibre (hay-first) diet, gradual diet changes, reduce weaning stress.
prognosis: Guarded in young rabbits; better with early aggressive support and fibre.
prevention: Hay-based diet from weaning, minimise stress, avoid abrupt diet changes and excess carbohydrate.

name: Osteoarthritis (Rabbit / Geriatric)
cause: Wear-and-tear degeneration of the joints (spine, hips, hocks) in middle-aged and older rabbits, worsened by obesity and inactivity. Because rabbits are prey animals they hide pain and almost never vocalise, so it is easily missed. The consequences are what owners usually notice: a rabbit that cannot posture to eat its caecotrophs (so a dirty bottom and urine scald appear), cannot groom (matting, dandruff), reduced jumping, and a hunched, reluctant posture.
script: A lot of older rabbits have arthritis, but because rabbits hide pain so well it often shows up indirectly — a mucky bottom because they can't twist round to eat their special night droppings, an ungroomed coat, less hopping, or just sitting hunched. It is one of the most under-diagnosed problems in older rabbits. We can help a great deal with safe pain relief, weight loss, and easy-access housing (low litter trays, soft flooring), which often transforms their comfort and tidiness.
preventableNote: Keep rabbits lean and active; soft flooring; not strictly preventable with age.
prognosis: Chronic but very manageable — quality of life improves markedly with pain control + weight/management.
prevention: Maintain healthy weight, exercise, non-slip soft flooring, accessible food/litter; assess older rabbits for hidden pain.

name: Tyzzer's Disease
cause: A severe bacterial gut infection caused by Clostridium piliforme, mostly striking young, recently-weaned or stressed small mammals. Spores are very hardy in the environment. It causes profuse, often watery/bloody diarrhoea, rapid dehydration, and frequently sudden death, with damage to the intestine, liver and heart. Outbreaks can move quickly through a group.
script: This is a serious bacterial infection of the gut that hits young or stressed rabbits and rodents hard, causing severe watery diarrhoea and very rapid decline — sadly some are found dead. It spreads through tough spores in the environment, so strict hygiene is vital. Treatment is intensive supportive care and antibiotics, but the outlook is guarded. The biggest levers are reducing stress around weaning and rigorous cleaning, because ordinary disinfectants don't all kill the spores (dilute bleach does).
preventableNote: Reduce weaning/transport stress; strict hygiene; 0.3% sodium hypochlorite (bleach) inactivates the spores.
prognosis: Grave — high mortality, often with sudden death; survivors need intensive care.
prevention: Minimise stress in young/weanling animals, excellent sanitation, isolate sick animals, bleach-based disinfection of housing.

name: Aberrant Conjunctival Overgrowth
cause: A rabbit-specific condition in which the conjunctiva (the pink membrane at the edge of the eye) grows as a sheet across the surface of the cornea from all directions, like a curtain closing over the eye — but, unlike a normal pterygium, it is not actually attached to the cornea underneath. The cause is unknown; young male dwarf rabbits are over-represented. Vision is obscured as the membrane advances. Simple trimming usually fails because it regrows.
script: A thin membrane from the edge of your rabbit's eye is growing inwards across the clear window, a bit like a curtain — it is a peculiar condition seen mostly in young dwarf rabbits. The good news is it is not painful or infectious and the cornea underneath is usually healthy. The catch is that simply cutting it away tends to let it grow back, so the surgery is a little more involved (tacking it back). We'll discuss whether it is advancing enough to need surgery or can be monitored.
preventableNote: Cause unknown; not preventable. Surgery (not simple resection) is needed if it threatens vision.
prognosis: Good for the eye itself; high recurrence after simple trimming, so a tacking technique is preferred.
prevention: None known. Monitor and refer for proper surgical correction if it advances over the pupil.

name: Dysautonomia (Rabbit)
cause: A poorly-understood failure of the autonomic ("automatic") nervous system that controls the gut and other organs — similar to grass sickness in horses. The gut effectively stops, so affected rabbits show severe GI stasis that does not respond to the usual treatment, along with a distended stomach, dry eyes/mucous membranes, and a dilated, poorly-emptying bladder. It is uncommon, hard to confirm in life, and carries a poor outlook.
script: This is an uncommon and serious condition where the "automatic" nerves that run the gut and bladder stop working, so the gut shuts down in a way that doesn't respond to the normal stasis treatment. It can be hard to confirm while the rabbit is alive, and unfortunately the outlook is poor. We treat supportively — fluids, feeding, keeping the bladder emptied — but we will be honest with you about the guarded prognosis as we work through it.
preventableNote: Cause unknown; not preventable.
prognosis: Poor — most cases do not recover; diagnosis is often confirmed only after death.
prevention: None established.

name: Cuterebra / Taenia serialis Cyst
cause: A lump under the skin caused by a parasite larva. Cuterebra is a botfly whose larva (a "warble") sits in a swelling with a breathing hole; it is mostly an outdoor/summer problem. Taenia serialis is a tapeworm of dogs/foxes whose intermediate larval stage (Coenurus) forms a fluid-filled cyst under a rabbit's skin or between muscles after the rabbit eats grass contaminated with dog/fox faeces. Both present as a soft swelling and are treated by careful surgical removal.
script: This lump is from a parasite larva living under the skin — either a bot-fly grub (which has a little breathing hole) or the larval stage of a tapeworm that the rabbit picked up from grass contaminated by dog or fox droppings. Neither is usually dangerous if dealt with properly, but the lump needs careful surgical removal — squeezing or rupturing it can cause a bad reaction. We'll also recommend worming the household dogs and keeping the rabbit's grazing area clear of dog/fox faeces.
preventableNote: Worm in-contact dogs; prevent access to areas soiled by dog/fox faeces; fly control + screens for Cuterebra.
prognosis: Good with complete surgical removal of the intact larva/cyst.
prevention: Deworm household dogs (Taenia), keep grazing free of dog/fox faeces, insect screens and fly control outdoors.

name: Iron Storage Disease (Hemochromatosis)
cause: Excess dietary iron is absorbed and deposited in the liver (and heart and other organs), eventually causing liver failure and fluid build-up in the body cavity. Certain birds are highly susceptible — toucans, toucanets, mynahs, birds of paradise, starlings and some lorikeets — especially on iron-rich pelleted or fruit diets. Signs are often vague (weight loss, lethargy, breathlessness from coelomic fluid) until advanced.
script: Your bird is one of the species that stores too much iron from the diet, and it has built up in the liver. It can be silent for a long time, then show as weight loss, low energy, or a swollen belly and laboured breathing as fluid collects. We can support it by draining fluid, sometimes removing small amounts of blood to lower iron, and — most importantly — switching to a low-iron diet and avoiding vitamin-C-rich foods that increase iron absorption. Prevention through the right diet is far better than treatment.
preventableNote: Largely preventable in susceptible species with a low-iron diet (<100 ppm) and by avoiding high vitamin C with meals.
prognosis: Guarded once liver disease/effusion is present; better if caught early and diet corrected.
prevention: Feed susceptible species (toucans, mynahs) a documented low-iron diet; avoid iron-fortified pellets and high-vitamin-C foods; monitor.

name: White Muscle Disease (Nutritional Myopathy)
cause: Degeneration of skeletal and heart muscle caused by deficiency of selenium and/or vitamin E. It is seen in fast-growing young animals, in birds and exotics fed frozen fish (freezing destroys vitamin E) or selenium-poor diets. Affected animals are weak, stiff, reluctant to move, and may suddenly die if the heart muscle is involved. Muscle looks pale ("white") at post-mortem.
script: Your animal's muscles — including possibly the heart — have been damaged by a lack of vitamin E and selenium in the diet. This shows as weakness, stiffness and reluctance to move, and in severe cases sudden collapse if the heart is affected. It is most common in animals fed frozen fish or unbalanced diets. We treat with vitamin E / selenium supplementation and correct the diet; the key for fish-eaters is adding vitamin E because freezing destroys it.
preventableNote: Preventable with adequate vitamin E + selenium; supplement vitamin E (~100 IU/kg fish) for frozen-fish/whole-prey feeders.
prognosis: Good if caught before heart involvement; cardiac cases may die suddenly.
prevention: Balanced diet with adequate selenium + vitamin E; supplement vitamin E for piscivores fed frozen fish; avoid rancid fats.

name: Nutritional Encephalomalacia
also: Vitamin E deficiency encephalopathy, "crazy chick disease"
cause: A brain disorder of young birds caused by vitamin E deficiency, in which the cerebellum (balance centre) is damaged. Affected chicks show incoordination, head tremors, falling over, twisted neck posture and an inability to stand, usually in the first weeks of life. Unlike white muscle disease, it is NOT corrected simply by adding selenium — vitamin E is the key.
script: This is a brain problem in young birds caused by a lack of vitamin E, which damages the balance centre of the brain — that is why you see the wobbling, head tremors and falling over. An important point is that, unlike the muscle form, this one is not fixed by selenium alone; vitamin E is what is needed. We supplement vitamin E and correct the diet, and prevention in the rest of the clutch/aviary is by ensuring fresh, vitamin-E-adequate feed.
preventableNote: Preventable with vitamin-E-adequate, fresh (non-rancid) starter diets.
prognosis: Fair if treated early; severe cerebellar damage may be permanent.
prevention: Fresh vitamin-E-adequate feed for growing birds; avoid rancid/old feed; supplement breeding/rearing diets.

name: Capture / Restraint Myopathy
cause: Muscle damage triggered by the extreme stress and exertion of capture, prolonged restraint, or chase — especially in flighty wild or zoo species (deer, antelope, hares, large birds). Adrenaline and over-worked muscles cause lactic acidosis and breakdown of muscle, releasing myoglobin that injures the kidneys. Animals may collapse, be unable to stand, or die hours-to-days after an apparently successful capture.
script: This is muscle damage brought on by the stress and struggle of being captured or restrained — it is a real danger in nervous wild or exotic species. The muscles (and sometimes the kidneys) are injured by the adrenaline surge and over-exertion, and animals can collapse or even die a day or two after a capture that seemed to go fine. Prevention is everything: short, calm, well-planned handling in cool conditions, and sometimes sedation, dramatically reduces the risk.
preventableNote: Largely preventable — minimise chase/restraint time, handle in cool weather, sedate, acclimatise, avoid repeat captures.
prognosis: Guarded once it develops; prevention is far more effective than treatment.
prevention: Low-stress capture (short duration, cool temperatures, visual cover, sedation); avoid over-handling flighty species.

name: Arginine Deficiency Encephalopathy
cause: Cats and birds cannot make their own arginine, an amino acid essential for the urea cycle that clears ammonia. A single arginine-deficient meal (e.g. a home-made or unbalanced diet) can cause ammonia to spike dangerously within hours, producing vomiting, drooling, tremors, seizures and even death. It is a true dietary emergency, classically described in cats fed arginine-free experimental diets.
script: Cats and birds depend completely on arginine in their food to clear ammonia from the body, and they can't make it themselves. If a meal is missing it, ammonia builds up fast and can cause drooling, tremors and seizures within hours — it is a genuine emergency. This is really a warning about unbalanced home-made diets. We treat by stabilising the seizures and ammonia and getting them back onto a complete, balanced diet; prevention is simply feeding a properly formulated food.
preventableNote: Entirely preventable — feed a complete, balanced commercial diet; avoid arginine-free home-made diets in cats/birds.
prognosis: Good if treated promptly; severe hyperammonemia can be fatal.
prevention: Feed complete balanced diets; never feed cats/birds long-term home-made or single-ingredient diets without formulation.

name: Hypervitaminosis D / Soft-tissue Mineralization
cause: Too much vitamin D (from over-supplementation, over-fortified diets, or rodenticide ingestion) drives blood calcium up, which then deposits in the kidneys, blood vessels and other soft tissues. Some fossorial/burrowing species are especially sensitive. Signs include increased drinking and urinating, weakness, anorexia and weight loss; severe cases develop kidney failure from calcium deposition.
script: Too much vitamin D — usually from over-supplementing or an over-fortified diet — has pushed the calcium level up, and it is being laid down in organs like the kidneys and blood vessels. That is why you may see extra drinking, low energy and poor appetite. We stop the source, support the kidneys, and lower the calcium. The lesson is that more is not better with vitamin/calcium supplements; they must be dosed correctly for the species.
preventableNote: Preventable by correct (not excessive) vitamin D/calcium supplementation; keep rodenticides away.
prognosis: Guarded if renal mineralization is established; better if caught early and source removed.
prevention: Dose vitamin D/calcium supplements correctly per species; avoid over-fortified diets; secure cholecalciferol rodenticides.

name: Grass Tetany (Hypomagnesemia)
cause: A sudden, life-threatening drop in blood magnesium, classically in lactating grazing herbivores (cattle, sheep, deer and grazing exotics) turned onto lush, fast-growing spring grass that is low in available magnesium. Low magnesium makes nerves and muscles hyper-excitable, causing muscle tremors, staggering, seizures and death, often very rapidly.
script: This is a sudden drop in magnesium in the blood, seen in grazing animals — especially nursing mothers — put onto lush young pasture. Low magnesium makes the muscles and nerves fire uncontrollably, so we see tremors, staggering and seizures, and it can be fatal very quickly. It is an emergency treated with magnesium (and calcium) supplementation, and prevented by supplementing magnesium and managing access to lush spring grass.
preventableNote: Preventable with magnesium supplementation and managed grazing of lush spring pasture, especially in lactating animals.
prognosis: Grave if untreated (rapid death); good if magnesium given promptly.
prevention: Magnesium supplementation, gradual introduction to lush pasture, extra care with lactating grazers.

name: Perosis (Slipped Tendon)
cause: A developmental leg deformity of growing birds caused by deficiency of manganese, choline, biotin, niacin or folic acid (and sometimes excess calcium/phosphorus). The growth plate of the hock develops abnormally, the leg bones bow and twist, and the large gastrocnemius (Achilles) tendon slips off the back of the hock, leaving the bird unable to stand normally on a splayed, deformed leg.
script: This leg deformity in a growing bird comes from a diet missing key nutrients — most importantly manganese, plus choline and certain B vitamins — during the period of rapid bone growth. The hock develops abnormally and the main tendon slips out of place, so the leg twists. Caught very early, correcting the diet can help; once the bones have deformed and the tendon has slipped, it is hard to fix. The real solution is a properly balanced growing diet for the whole clutch.
preventableNote: Preventable with a balanced growing diet (adequate manganese, choline, biotin) and correct Ca:P; not over-supplementing calcium.
prognosis: Guarded once deformity is established; early nutritional cases may improve.
prevention: Feed breeding/growing birds a complete balanced diet with adequate manganese + B vitamins; avoid all-seed or unbalanced hand-rearing formulas.

name: Sebaceous Adenitis (Rabbit)
cause: An uncommon skin condition in which the oil (sebaceous) glands of the skin are destroyed, leading to widespread scaling, crusting and hair loss, typically starting over the head/neck and spreading. In rabbits it has an important association: a number of cases are a paraneoplastic sign of an underlying THYMOMA, so a rabbit with this exfoliative skin disease should have its chest checked.
script: Your rabbit has a skin condition where the oil glands are being lost, giving widespread flaking, crusting and hair loss. In rabbits there is an important link we always check for: this kind of skin disease can be a signal of a chest tumour (thymoma), so we usually recommend a chest X-ray. Treatment is aimed at the skin (and the underlying cause if found); it tends to be a managed, long-term condition rather than a quick cure.
preventableNote: Not preventable; important to screen for underlying thymoma.
prognosis: Chronic; depends on whether an underlying thymoma is present and treatable.
prevention: None; investigate the chest (thymoma) in any rabbit with exfoliative skin disease.

name: EEHV (Elephant Endotheliotropic Herpesvirus)
cause: A herpesvirus that causes acute, frequently fatal haemorrhagic disease in young (often weanling) elephants, especially Asian elephants. The virus damages small blood vessels throughout the body, causing internal bleeding, fluid build-up around the heart, and rapid collapse. Diagnosis is by PCR on blood, and early aggressive antiviral and supportive treatment offers the only chance of survival — making it the single biggest infectious-disease concern in elephant calves.
script: This is a herpesvirus that causes a sudden, severe bleeding disease in young elephants and is the leading cause of death in elephant calves. It moves fast, so the key is early detection — many programmes screen calves' blood regularly by PCR so treatment can start at the very first sign. Treatment is antiviral drugs plus intensive supportive care. We mention it because in young elephants any lethargy or swelling must be taken extremely seriously and tested immediately.
preventableNote: No vaccine yet; survival depends on PCR surveillance of at-risk calves + immediate antiviral/supportive treatment.
prognosis: Grave — high mortality even with treatment; best chance with very early PCR-based detection.
prevention: Routine PCR monitoring of young elephants in managed herds; immediate treatment at first signs; biosecurity.

name: Helicobacter Gastritis (Ferret)
cause: *Helicobacter mustelae* is a spiral bacterium that colonizes the stomach of nearly every ferret early in life. Usually silent, but stress or a concurrent illness lets it drive chronic gastritis and stomach/duodenal ulcers. Signs are intermittent nausea and vomiting, teeth grinding (bruxism), pawing at the mouth, drooling, weight loss, and black tarry stool (melena) from a bleeding ulcer. It is the ferret equivalent of *H. pylori* in people.
script: Almost all ferrets carry a stomach bacterium called Helicobacter — usually harmlessly. Stress or another illness can let it inflame the stomach lining and cause ulcers, which is behind the nausea, the teeth grinding, and any dark tarry stool you have seen. The good news is it responds well to a 3-week "triple therapy" — two antibiotics plus a stomach-acid reducer — together with treating whatever stress set it off.
preventableNote: Carriage is near-universal and cannot be eliminated. Flare-ups are reduced by minimizing stress, treating concurrent disease early, and never giving NSAIDs/steroids on an empty stomach.
prognosis: Good with triple therapy (amoxicillin + metronidazole + bismuth, often with a proton-pump inhibitor) for ~3 weeks. Relapse is possible; actively bleeding ulcers are more guarded.
prevention: Minimize stress, treat concurrent illness, gastroprotection (sucralfate / PPI) when using ulcerogenic drugs, and address dental/dietary contributors.

name: Mycobacteriosis (Avian/Reptile)
also: Avian tuberculosis, M. avium complex, M. marinum, fish-tank granuloma
cause: Chronic infection with slow-growing acid-fast *Mycobacterium* species — *M. avium* complex in birds; *M. marinum / M. chelonae* in reptiles, fish and amphibians. They form granulomas in the gut, liver, spleen and skin. Birds show chronic wasting despite a good appetite, intermittent diarrhea, and dullness; reptiles/fish develop firm skin nodules and internal granulomas. ⚠ ZOONOTIC — *M. marinum* causes "fish-tank granuloma" skin nodules in people who handle tanks; *M. avium* is a risk to immunocompromised owners.
script: This is a slow, chronic bacterial infection — a relative of tuberculosis. It builds up gradually, often as steady weight loss even while the animal is still eating, or as firm skin lumps. Importantly, it can spread to people — especially anyone with a weakened immune system — so we handle it carefully with gloves and good hygiene. Diagnosis needs special acid-fast stains, culture or PCR, and treatment is long and not always successful, so we will talk through the options honestly.
preventableNote: No vaccine. Risk is reduced by quarantine, clean water systems (especially for fish/amphibians), and good hygiene. Often acquired from the environment or contaminated water.
prognosis: Guarded — usually advanced by diagnosis; requires prolonged multi-drug therapy and, given the zoonotic risk, euthanasia is often considered, particularly in households with immunocompromised people.
prevention: Quarantine and test new animals; maintain clean water systems; wear gloves and wash hands; remove/cull-test affected animals in collections.

name: Salmonellosis (Reptile)
cause: *Salmonella enterica* lives in the gut of most healthy reptiles (turtles, lizards, snakes) and is shed intermittently in the feces, usually WITHOUT making the reptile sick. Overt disease (enteritis, septicemia) occurs mainly in young, stressed or immunosuppressed reptiles. ⚠ The major issue is ZOONOSIS — reptile-associated salmonellosis is a well-recognized cause of serious gut infection in people, especially young children, the elderly and the immunocompromised, spread by hand-to-mouth contact.
script: Most reptiles naturally carry Salmonella in their gut without being sick — it comes and goes in their droppings, so a "negative" test does not mean it is gone. The real concern is people: it can cause serious illness, especially in young children, the elderly, or anyone with a weak immune system. The key is hygiene — wash hands thoroughly after handling your reptile or cleaning its tank, keep reptiles away from infants, and never clean equipment in the kitchen sink. If the reptile itself is unwell (off food, runny stool), we will treat and support it.
preventableNote: Carriage cannot be reliably cleared (antibiotics do not eliminate it and promote resistance). Prevention focuses on hygiene to protect people, not on "treating" the reptile.
prognosis: Reptile is usually a subclinical carrier (good); clinical septicemic cases are guarded. Human infections can be severe and are the main reason this matters.
prevention: Strict hand hygiene after contact; keep reptiles away from children under 5, the elderly and the immunocompromised; never wash enclosures/equipment in food-prep areas; supervise children.

name: Haemoparasitosis (Avian/Reptile)
also: Avian malaria, Plasmodium, Haemoproteus, Leucocytozoon, haemogregarines
cause: Blood-borne protozoa transmitted by biting insects — mosquitoes, biting midges, blackflies and sandflies. *Plasmodium* (avian malaria), *Haemoproteus* and *Leucocytozoon* infect birds; haemogregarines infect reptiles. They parasitize red blood cells and tissues, causing anemia, weakness, lethargy and pale or yellow mucous membranes, and sometimes sudden death — especially in naive birds such as penguins and raptors exposed to avian malaria. Diagnosis is by examining a stained blood smear.
script: Your animal has a blood parasite spread by biting insects like mosquitoes. It infects the blood cells and causes anemia — which is why we see paleness, weakness and low energy. Some species are very vulnerable and can deteriorate quickly. We confirm it by looking at a blood smear under the microscope. Treatment uses antimalarial-type drugs (often chloroquine combined with primaquine), and just as important is insect control — screening the enclosure and removing standing water.
preventableNote: No vaccine. Reduce vector exposure: mosquito netting/insect screening, remove standing water, and protect high-risk birds at dusk/dawn.
prognosis: Variable — many tolerate low parasite loads; naive or heavily-infected birds (avian malaria in penguins/raptors) can die. Better with early treatment.
prevention: Vector control — insect-screen enclosures, eliminate standing water, and house high-risk birds indoors during mosquito season.

name: Corneal Malacia (Melting Ulcer)
cause: A corneal ulcer that is actively "melting": enzymes (proteases/collagenases) released by bacteria — especially *Pseudomonas* — and by the body's own white blood cells rapidly dissolve the collagen of the corneal stroma. The cornea becomes soft and grey-gelatinous and can perforate within hours to a day or two. It is an ophthalmic EMERGENCY, usually arising from an untreated or infected corneal ulcer or trauma.
script: This is the most urgent kind of eye ulcer — the surface of the eye is literally melting because infection and inflammation are dissolving its collagen. It can rupture the eye within a day if we do not act fast. We treat intensively: very frequent antibiotic drops, anti-melting ("anti-collagenase") drops such as serum, pain relief, and sometimes urgent surgery (a graft) to save the eye. Please give the drops exactly on schedule — sometimes every 1–2 hours — because the timing is what saves the eye.
preventableNote: Largely preventable by treating any corneal ulcer promptly and NEVER using steroid drops on an ulcer. Some trauma is unavoidable.
prognosis: Guarded — globe-threatening. Aggressive medical ± surgical therapy (conjunctival graft) saves many eyes if started early; perforation may require enucleation.
prevention: Treat corneal ulcers early; never put steroid drops on an ulcer; use an Elizabethan collar; treat *Pseudomonas* with culture-guided antibiotics.

name: Keratomycosis (Fungal Keratitis)
cause: A fungal infection of the cornea (*Aspergillus*, *Fusarium*, *Candida*), usually following corneal trauma from plant material or after prolonged topical antibiotic/steroid use that lets fungi overgrow. It produces a stubborn, slowly progressive corneal ulcer with feathery white infiltrates that does NOT respond to antibacterial drops. Uncommon but important — it needs antifungal therapy and a different approach from a routine bacterial ulcer.
script: This eye ulcer is caused by a fungus, not ordinary bacteria — which is why it has not responded to standard antibiotic drops. Fungal eye infections are stubborn and slow, so treatment is long (often weeks) with special antifungal eye drops such as natamycin or voriconazole, and we monitor by checking samples from the eye for fungal threads. We also stop any steroid drops, which actually feed the fungus.
preventableNote: Reduce risk by avoiding prolonged broad-spectrum topical antibiotics/steroids on the eye and by protecting the eye from plant/organic trauma.
prognosis: Guarded — slow to clear; may need months of antifungals ± surgery. Better the earlier it is recognized (cytology on any ulcer that will not heal).
prevention: Do not over-use topical antibiotics/steroids; treat corneal trauma promptly; take corneal cytology if an ulcer fails to heal on antibacterials.

name: Nutritional Secondary Hyperparathyroidism (NSHP)
cause: Caused by a diet too low in calcium and/or too high in phosphorus, and/or a lack of vitamin D3/UVB. When blood calcium drops, the parathyroid glands release hormone that pulls calcium OUT of the bones to keep the blood level safe. Over weeks to months the bones become soft and bendy (fibrous osteodystrophy) → bowed limbs, folding (pathologic) fractures, a soft rubbery jaw, and muscle tremors or seizures once blood calcium finally falls. Classic in all-seed parrots, insectivorous reptiles fed unsupplemented insects (mealworm Ca:P is ~1:14!), all-meat-fed carnivores, and fast-growing young animals.
script: This is a bone disease caused by diet — too little calcium, too much phosphorus, or not enough vitamin D/UVB light. When calcium runs short, the body steals it from the bones to keep the blood level safe, and the bones slowly go soft. That is why you may see bent limbs, a rubbery jaw, reluctance to move, or even tremors. The encouraging part is it is almost entirely preventable and often reversible if we catch it early — we correct the diet (calcium and the calcium-to-phosphorus balance), add vitamin D3 and proper UVB lighting for reptiles and birds, and support the bones while they heal.
preventableNote: Almost entirely diet/husbandry-preventable: correct dietary Ca:P (target 1:1–2:1), calcium-dust and gut-load feeder insects, provide unfiltered UVB plus dietary D3 for reptiles/birds, and avoid all-seed or all-meat diets.
prognosis: Good if caught early and the diet/husbandry is corrected (often reversible in young animals). Established deformities and healed pathologic fractures are permanent; an acute hypocalcemic seizure is an emergency.
prevention: Balanced diet with Ca:P 1:1–2:1; calcium-dust/gut-load feeder insects; correct unfiltered UVB lighting + dietary D3 for reptiles & birds; avoid all-seed parrot and all-meat carnivore diets; supplement calcium in laying females. Ca:P of common feeders (gut-load to fix): mealworm 1:14, cricket 1:7, waxworm 1:13 — all calcium-poor; dust before feeding. Note: vitamin D3 is ≥8× more potent than D2, and fish/amphibians/reptiles/birds can use ONLY D3; UVB passes through vinyl, cellulose triacetate, plexiglass G-UVT & Teflon but is BLOCKED by ordinary window glass, acrylic and polycarbonate.

name: Zinc Deficiency
cause: Too little dietary zinc — or, commonly, over-supplementing CALCIUM on a meat/grain diet, which blocks zinc uptake. Zinc is essential for skin, feather and bone formation. Deficiency causes abnormally thickened, flaky skin (parakeratosis), poor or frayed feathering, hair loss and slow wound healing; in poultry it produces "swollen hock syndrome" with short, thick leg bones. Requirements vary widely (≈10–70 ppm; marmosets need up to ~190 ppm).
script: This is a skin, coat and feather problem caused by too little zinc — or by over-supplementing calcium, which actually blocks zinc absorption. You may see thickened flaky skin, poor feathering or a dull coat, and slow-healing wounds. We correct it by balancing the diet and the calcium-to-zinc ratio, and the skin and feathering improve as new tissue grows in.
preventableNote: Diet-preventable — feed a balanced, species-appropriate diet and avoid over-supplementing calcium (which antagonizes zinc).
prognosis: Good — skin/coat recovers with dietary correction as new tissue grows in; established bone deformities (swollen hock) may persist.
prevention: Balanced complete diet; do not over-supplement calcium on meat/grain diets; ensure adequate zinc for high-requirement species (e.g. marmosets).

name: Copper Deficiency
cause: Inadequate dietary copper, or excess zinc/calcium antagonizing it — classically in cats fed calcium-fortified red-meat diets. Copper is needed for pigment, red-blood-cell formation and strong connective tissue. Deficiency causes faded coat or feather colour (achromotrichia), anaemia and weakness; in ruminants it adds bone fractures, abnormal hoof/antler growth, ataxia and poor fertility, and it can weaken the aorta and connective tissue.
script: Your animal's faded coat colour and low energy can come from too little copper in the diet — or from too much calcium or zinc, which block copper. Copper is needed for pigment, healthy blood and strong tissues. We confirm with bloodwork and correct the diet; colour and energy improve as the body rebuilds its stores.
preventableNote: Diet-preventable — feed a balanced diet and avoid the calcium-fortified all-meat diets that induce it.
prognosis: Good with dietary correction; severe connective-tissue/vascular damage may be permanent.
prevention: Balanced diet with adequate copper; avoid over-supplementing calcium or zinc; treat the underlying feeding pattern.

name: Sodium / Potassium Deficiency
cause: Inadequate dietary electrolytes. SODIUM: too little salt, or — in fish-eaters/marine mammals — feeding fish thawed in tap water (sodium leaches ~25% out within 3 hours); causes poor growth, weakness, incoordination, corneal keratinization and, severely, shock. POTASSIUM: causes muscle weakness, poor gut tone with intestinal distension (bloat), cardiac and respiratory weakness, and renal tubular degeneration.
script: This is an electrolyte (salt/potassium) shortage from the diet. It shows up as weakness, wobbliness and poor growth, and if severe it can affect the heart and gut. A common hidden cause in fish-eaters is thawing fish in tap water, which leaches the salt out. We correct it by adjusting the diet and, if the animal is unwell, with supportive fluids.
preventableNote: Diet-preventable — adequate dietary salt (≥3 g NaCl/kg food in at-risk feeders), and never thaw feeder fish in plain tap water.
prognosis: Good if corrected before collapse; severe hypokalemia with cardiac/renal involvement is more guarded.
prevention: Balanced electrolytes in the diet; thaw feeder fish in saline-supplemented water; supplement at-risk piscivores.

name: Selenium Toxicity
cause: Excess selenium — over-supplementation (the safe margin is narrow) or grazing seleniferous plants/soils. Acute toxicity causes "blind staggers" (neurological — ataxia, apparent blindness, star-gazing); chronic toxicity ("alkali disease" in livestock) causes hair/feather loss, lameness and abnormal hoof growth. It is the over-dose counterpart of selenium DEFICIENCY (White Muscle Disease) — both are dangerous, so supplement carefully.
script: This is selenium poisoning — too much of a mineral that is actually essential in tiny amounts. The safe range is narrow, so over-supplementing, or grazing high-selenium plants, can tip into toxicity, causing wobbliness, apparent blindness, and hair or feather loss. We stop the selenium source and give supportive care; prevention is all about careful, measured supplementation.
preventableNote: Preventable with careful dosing — never over-supplement selenium; know your local soil/forage selenium levels.
prognosis: Guarded once neurological signs appear; better if the source is removed early.
prevention: Measured selenium supplementation only; avoid seleniferous forage; double-check compounded mineral mixes.

name: Hypovitaminosis B-complex
cause: Deficiency of B-vitamins, each with a signature picture. THIAMINE (B1): destroyed by thiaminase in raw fish/shellfish (90 min at 37 °C) and by bracken/horsetail — fish-eaters and nectivores get neurological signs, head tremor, seizures. RIBOFLAVIN (B2): "curled-toe paralysis" in birds. B6: anaemia and peripheral neuritis. BIOTIN (B7): blocked by avidin in RAW egg white → "Fatty Liver and Kidney Syndrome" (poultry), dermatitis, alopecia.
script: This is a B-vitamin shortage. The commonest exotic version is thiamine (B1) deficiency in fish-eating animals, because raw fish contains an enzyme that destroys B1 — it can cause tremors and seizures. Other B-vitamins cause curled toes (birds), anaemia, or a fatty-liver/skin syndrome (from too much raw egg white). The good news is the response to the right B-vitamin is often dramatic — thiamine can reverse signs within hours.
preventableNote: Diet-preventable — supplement thiamine for fish/whole-prey feeders (≈1–2 mg/kg diet; affected pinnipeds 35 mg/day), avoid feeding large amounts of raw egg white, and provide a complete B-vitamin diet.
prognosis: Excellent for thiamine deficiency (often resolves within 6–12 h of B1); other B-deficiencies recover with supplementation if not advanced.
prevention: Thiamine-supplement frozen-fish/whole-prey diets; never freeze-and-feed fish without B1; avoid raw-egg-white-heavy diets; feed complete B-vitamin sources.

name: Essential Fatty Acid Deficiency
cause: A diet low in essential fatty acids, or fats that have gone rancid/oxidized. Cats (and other strict carnivores) cannot make arachidonic acid from plant linoleic acid, so they need an animal source. Deficiency causes a dull, dry, flaky coat, hair/feather loss, poor wound healing, slow growth, fatty liver, and impaired immunity and reproduction — a striking example is a cheetah held in 2-year anestrus on a lean-meat diet that resolved after adding fish oil and evening primrose oil. Requirement is ≈1–2% of total calories.
script: Your animal's dry, dull, flaky coat and poor condition can come from a shortage of essential fatty acids — the "good fats" the diet must supply. Strict carnivores like cats especially need an animal-source fat. We add the right fats (such as fish oil) and improve the overall diet; the coat, skin and general condition usually improve over several weeks, and it can even restore fertility.
preventableNote: Diet-preventable — include ≈1–2% of calories as essential fatty acids from an appropriate (often animal) source; avoid rancid/oxidized fats and lean-meat-only diets.
prognosis: Good — coat, skin and reproductive function generally recover over weeks once the diet is corrected.
prevention: Balanced fat in the diet (fish oil / appropriate animal fats for carnivores); store fats properly to avoid rancidity; avoid lean-meat-only feeding.

name: Trichoblastoma (Rabbit)
also: Basal cell tumour, commonest rabbit skin tumour
cause: The single most common skin tumour of pet rabbits, arising from the germinative (hair-follicle) cells of the skin. It is usually a benign, firm, well-defined lump in or under the skin of a middle-aged to older rabbit (3–12 years). It grows slowly and very rarely spreads, but a larger one can ulcerate. Surgical removal is curative.
script: This is the most common skin lump in pet rabbits — it grows from the cells at the base of the hair follicles. The good news is it is almost always benign: it stays local and rarely spreads anywhere else. We remove it surgically, which is usually curative, and send it to the lab to confirm exactly what it is. Finding a new lump and having it checked, like you did, is exactly the right thing to do.
preventableNote: Not preventable — a sporadic tumour of ageing skin. Early removal gives the best outcome and the simplest surgery.
prognosis: Excellent — benign; surgical excision is usually curative.
prevention: None specific. Check older rabbits for new skin lumps at routine visits so they can be removed while small.

name: Squamous Cell Carcinoma (Exotic)
cause: A locally aggressive malignant tumour of skin/mucosal surface cells. Favoured sites: the hedgehog ORAL cavity (gums, jaw) and pinna; bird mucocutaneous junctions (beak, eyelids, uropygial gland, mouth); the sugar-glider cloacal area. It invades surrounding tissue and bone; distant spread is uncommon but local destruction is severe. Chronic UV exposure or chronic irritation are risk factors. Hedgehogs are especially prone — oral SCC presents as a non-healing mouth mass with drooling, dropped food and weight loss.
script: This is a skin-and-mouth cancer that tends to dig into the nearby tissue rather than spread to distant organs. In hedgehogs it very often starts in the mouth, which is why you may see drooling, trouble eating, or weight loss. Because it invades locally, the best results come from catching it early and removing it widely — sometimes with extra local treatments. We will take a biopsy to confirm it and plan the best option from there.
preventableNote: Partly — limit chronic UV exposure and chronic irritation; most cases are sporadic, so early detection of a non-healing lump is the main lever.
prognosis: Guarded — locally aggressive and often advanced by diagnosis (especially hedgehog oral SCC). Wide excision / electrochemotherapy / intralesional therapy can help; metastasis is uncommon.
prevention: Limit excess UV; check the mouth and skin of older hedgehogs and birds; biopsy any non-healing lump or mouth mass early.

name: Hamster Lymphoma
cause: The most common cancer of pet hamsters, frequently associated with hamster polyomavirus (HaPV). There are two forms: an internal/abdominal lymphoma (typically 4–12 months old) and a cutaneous epitheliotropic T-cell lymphoma (6–23 months) that causes patchy hair loss, scaly/crusty skin and skin lumps. Most are T-cell. It is progressive; chemotherapy (prednisolone ± chlorambucil/lomustine/L-asparaginase) may slow it but the prognosis is guarded.
script: This is the most common cancer in hamsters, and it is often linked to a hamster-specific virus. It can appear inside the belly or in the skin — as hair loss, scaly patches, or lumps. Hamsters are very small and the disease tends to move quickly, so treatment is mostly about comfort and slowing it down rather than a cure. We will confirm it with a sample and focus on keeping your hamster comfortable and eating well.
preventableNote: The viral (HaPV) form is contagious between hamsters — isolate affected animals and disinfect. It is not otherwise preventable.
prognosis: Guarded to poor — progressive; chemotherapy may give temporary remission, but the hamster's small size limits options.
prevention: Isolate affected hamsters (HaPV spreads between them); good hygiene and quarantine in colonies and breeding groups.

name: Anticoagulant Rodenticide Toxicosis
cause: The pet eats rat-bait (or a poisoned rodent). These rodenticides block vitamin-K recycling, so the body runs out of working clotting factors after 2–5 days and bleeds — often INTERNALLY, where you can't see it: into the chest (sudden breathlessness), abdomen, joints or gut. Outward signs are pale gums, weakness, bruising, nosebleeds, blood in urine/stool, or coughing blood.
script: Your pet swallowed rat poison that stops the blood from clotting. The scary part is the bleeding often starts a few days later and can be hidden inside the body, so we act now even if it looks okay. The antidote is vitamin K1, usually for 3–6 weeks, and if it is already bleeding we may need a plasma or blood transfusion and oxygen. With early treatment most pets recover fully.
preventableNote: 100% preventable — store all rodenticides where pets can never reach them; tell us the exact product/active ingredient if you have the box.
prognosis: Excellent if treated before major bleeding; guarded once bleeding into the chest/brain has occurred.
prevention: Keep rat-bait sealed + out of reach; pick up dead rodents; bring the product packaging to the clinic so we choose the right length of vitamin-K course.

name: Bromethalin Rodenticide Toxicosis
cause: A newer rat-bait that is NOT an anticoagulant — there is no vitamin-K antidote. Bromethalin poisons the nerve cells' energy supply, causing fluid to build up in the brain and spinal cord. Cats are especially sensitive. Signs are muscle tremors, hyper-excitability, wobbliness, seizures and high body temperature, often within 4–18 hours of a big dose (or days later with smaller amounts).
script: This rat poison attacks the nervous system, not the blood — so the vitamin K used for the common rat poisons will NOT work here. There is no specific antidote, so the key is decontaminating early (making the pet vomit + repeated charcoal) and supporting the brain (controlling swelling with mannitol, controlling tremors and seizures). Cats are very sensitive. Bringing the box so we know exactly which poison it is makes a big difference.
preventableNote: Preventable — secure all baits. Knowing the active ingredient is critical because the treatment is completely different from anticoagulant baits.
prognosis: Guarded — depends on dose; mild cases recover with decontamination, severe neurological cases are often fatal.
prevention: Lock away rodenticides; identify the active ingredient on the package; early decontamination is the best lever.

name: Cholecalciferol Rodenticide Toxicosis
cause: A rat-bait that is concentrated vitamin D3. It drives blood calcium and phosphorus dangerously high, which then mineralises (turns to "stone") the kidneys, heart and gut over days — causing kidney failure. Early signs are vague: increased thirst/urination, poor appetite, vomiting, weakness.
script: This rat poison is basically a huge overdose of vitamin D. It pushes calcium so high that it can permanently damage the kidneys over the next few days, so we treat aggressively even before the pet looks very sick. Treatment is several days of IV fluids and special drugs to bring calcium down, plus repeated blood tests. It is one of the more dangerous rat poisons because the damage is to the kidneys.
preventableNote: Preventable — secure baits. There is no simple antidote, so early aggressive treatment + monitoring calcium/phosphorus is essential.
prognosis: Guarded — high calcium that has begun mineralising the kidneys carries a poor prognosis; better if caught early.
prevention: Keep rodenticides locked away; identify the product; early veterinary care + days of calcium monitoring.

name: Organophosphate / Carbamate Toxicosis
also: OP / carbamate insecticide poisoning, AChE inhibitor
cause: Poisoning by agricultural/household insecticides, some flea products, and ant/roach baits. These block the enzyme that switches off nerve signals, so the body floods with acetylcholine. Classic "SLUDGE" signs appear: Salivation, Lacrimation, Urination, Defecation, GI cramping, Emesis — plus muscle tremors/twitching, pinpoint pupils, and in severe cases seizures and breathing failure. Cats are more susceptible.
script: Your pet was poisoned by an insecticide that makes the nerves "overfire" — that's why we see drooling, tearing, vomiting, diarrhoea, tremors and pinpoint pupils. The antidote is atropine to dry up those secretions and protect the heart, plus (for organophosphates) a second drug called pralidoxime, given early, that frees up the poisoned enzyme. We also bathe off any skin contamination. With prompt treatment many pets do well.
preventableNote: Preventable — store/use insecticides correctly; never apply agricultural OPs to pets; keep pets out of freshly treated areas.
prognosis: Good with early atropine ± pralidoxime; guarded if seizures/respiratory failure or delayed treatment.
prevention: Safe storage + use of insecticides; keep pets off treated fields/surfaces; know the product (OP vs carbamate changes the antidote choice).

name: Pyrethroid Toxicosis (Permethrin — Cat)
cause: Cats poisoned by concentrated permethrin/pyrethroid flea products — most often a DOG spot-on wrongly applied to a cat, or a cat grooming a treated dog. Cats can't metabolise it well. It over-stimulates nerves, causing intense whole-body muscle TREMORS, twitching, drooling, hyper-excitability, wobbliness and seizures, often with a high temperature.
script: Your cat reacted to a flea product containing permethrin — cats are very sensitive to it, and dog flea treatments are a common cause. It causes severe muscle tremors and can progress to seizures. There is no single antidote, so we bathe off the product, give a muscle relaxant (methocarbamol) for the tremors, control seizures, and support temperature and fluids. Most cats recover well with prompt, intensive care over 1–3 days.
preventableNote: Almost entirely preventable — NEVER use dog (permethrin) flea products on cats; keep treated dogs away from cats until dry.
prognosis: Good with prompt decontamination + methocarbamol; guarded if prolonged seizures/hyperthermia.
prevention: Use only cat-labelled flea products; separate cats from freshly-treated dogs; read every flea-product label.

name: Paracetamol (Acetaminophen) Toxicosis
cause: Poisoning from human paracetamol — given by a well-meaning owner or stolen. CATS are exquisitely sensitive (they lack the liver enzyme to detoxify it): even one tablet can be fatal, causing the blood pigment to convert to a useless form (methemoglobinemia) → brown blood, blue-grey gums, breathlessness, and swelling of the face/paws, plus liver damage. Dogs mainly get liver injury at higher doses.
script: Paracetamol is very poisonous to pets — especially cats, where even a single tablet can be deadly. It turns the blood a brownish colour so it can't carry oxygen (that's the blue-grey gums and laboured breathing) and damages the liver. The antidote is N-acetylcysteine, and the sooner we start it the better, along with oxygen and liver support. Please never give human painkillers to pets — let us prescribe a safe one.
preventableNote: 100% preventable — NEVER give paracetamol (or other human painkillers) to cats or dogs; store human medicines out of reach.
prognosis: Fair if N-acetylcysteine started early; guarded with severe methemoglobinemia or liver failure (worse in cats).
prevention: Never give human analgesics to pets; keep medicines secured; call us before giving ANY medication.

name: Ethylene Glycol Toxicosis
cause: The pet drinks antifreeze/radiator coolant — it tastes sweet. The body turns it into crystals (calcium oxalate) that destroy the kidneys within 1–3 days. Early (hours): the pet seems "drunk" — wobbly, vomiting, drinking/urinating a lot. Then a deceptive "better" phase. Then sudden kidney failure with little/no urine. Cats need only a tiny amount.
script: Your pet drank antifreeze, which is sweet but extremely toxic to the kidneys. The catch is timing: the antidote (fomepizole, or an ethanol drip) only works if we give it very early — within a few hours — before the poison has turned into kidney-destroying crystals. After that, the kidneys may already be failing and treatment becomes much harder (sometimes dialysis). That's why this is a race-against-the-clock emergency.
preventableNote: Preventable — store coolant sealed, clean up spills immediately (a teaspoon can kill a cat), use pet-safer propylene-glycol products.
prognosis: Good ONLY if the antidote is started very early (dog <8–12h, cat <3h); grave once anuric kidney failure is established.
prevention: Lock away antifreeze; wipe up garage/driveway spills at once; switch to propylene-glycol coolant where possible.

name: Grape / Raisin Toxicosis
cause: Dogs that eat grapes, raisins, sultanas or currants (including in baked goods) can develop acute kidney injury. The toxic dose is unpredictable — some dogs are affected by a small handful, others tolerate more — so ALL ingestions are treated seriously. Signs start with vomiting, then poor appetite, lethargy, and over 1–3 days reduced/absent urine as the kidneys fail.
script: Grapes and raisins can cause sudden kidney failure in dogs, and we can't predict how much is dangerous for an individual dog — so we treat every grape/raisin ingestion seriously. If it was recent we make your dog vomit and give charcoal, then run 2–3 days of IV fluids to protect the kidneys and monitor blood tests. Caught early, most dogs do very well; the danger is when the kidneys have already shut down.
preventableNote: Preventable — keep grapes/raisins (and raisin-containing foods like fruit cake, trail mix, hot-cross buns) away from dogs.
prognosis: Good if decontaminated + fluids started early; guarded once the dog stops producing urine.
prevention: No grapes/raisins/currants for dogs; mind baked goods + cereal bars; induce-vomiting + fluids early on any ingestion.

name: Snake Envenomation
cause: A venomous snakebite — common in Thailand. The effects depend on the snake: cobras/kraits (Elapidae) cause nerve paralysis + tissue death; vipers (e.g. green pit viper) cause swelling, tissue necrosis and bleeding/clotting failure; sea-snakes cause muscle breakdown. Typical findings are a painful swollen bite site (often a limb/face), bleeding, collapse, and — with neurotoxic snakes — weakness progressing to breathing paralysis.
script: Your pet was bitten by a venomous snake. The right treatment depends on the type of snake, so any clue helps (a photo from a safe distance is ideal — but do not chase or handle it). Antivenin is the real treatment and works best given early, along with pain relief, IV fluids, and — for paralysing snakes — possibly help with breathing. Keep your pet as calm and still as possible on the way in; that slows the venom. Do NOT use a tourniquet or cut the wound.
preventableNote: Reduce risk — keep grass short, clear rubble/wood piles, leash dogs on walks, avoid dawn/dusk in snake areas. Bites can't always be avoided.
prognosis: Variable — good with early antivenin + support; guarded with large venom load, neurotoxic paralysis, or delayed treatment.
prevention: Snake-proof the yard (short grass, no debris piles), supervise/leash outdoors, know the local venomous species; seek care immediately + keep the pet still.

name: Toad Toxin Toxicosis
cause: The pet mouths or eats a toad (cane/bufo and relatives). Their skin glands secrete bufotoxins — natural cardiac glycosides (like an overdose of heart medication). It is absorbed straight through the mouth lining: immediate profuse drooling and pawing at the mouth, then wobbliness, seizures, and dangerous heart-rhythm changes; severe cases can die.
script: Your pet got toad toxin in its mouth — these toads secrete a poison that acts on the heart. The very first thing to do (even at home) is to flush the mouth with lots of clean running water for several minutes, wiping the gums, being careful the pet doesn't swallow it. Then we control the drooling, seizures and especially the heart rhythm. Mild cases that get a good mouth-rinse early often do well; severe heart effects are dangerous.
preventableNote: Reduce risk — supervise pets outdoors at night (when toads are active), remove water bowls/lights that attract toads, train "leave it".
prognosis: Good for mild cases with prompt mouth-flushing; guarded with seizures or cardiac arrhythmias (cane/bufo toads are more dangerous).
prevention: Supervise at dusk/night, remove outdoor pet food/water that draws toads, fence ponds; flush the mouth immediately on any contact.

name: Zinc Phosphide Rodenticide Toxicosis
cause: A rat/mole bait that releases toxic PHOSPHINE gas when it meets stomach acid — so a fed stomach makes it worse. There is no antidote. Signs come on fast: vomiting (the vomit smells of "rotten fish/garlic"), bloating, abdominal pain, then breathing distress, seizures and collapse from lung + liver + heart damage. ⚠ The released gas + vomit are hazardous to PEOPLE too.
script: This bait turns into a poisonous gas inside the stomach, and there is no specific antidote — so we act fast with decontamination and intensive support. Important safety note: the gas and your pet's vomit can affect people, so we manage it in a well-ventilated area. We give antacids to slow the gas, charcoal, IV fluids and liver protection, and control seizures. Please tell us the product so we're certain which poison it is.
preventableNote: Preventable — secure all baits; if ingestion is suspected, decontaminate in a ventilated space (the phosphine gas is a human hazard too).
prognosis: Guarded — rapid onset, no antidote; mild cases survive with early support, severe multi-organ cases are often fatal.
prevention: Lock away zinc-phosphide/mole baits; ventilate during decontamination; identify the product; seek immediate care.
